EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota. While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across southeast South Dakota, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts were mainly 1 to 2 inches across far east and southeast South Dakota, and 3 to 5 inches from south central South Dakota into east central South Dakota. As a result of the snowfall rate, roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across southeast South Dakota, with four to six hours of moderate snowfall during the 0800CST to 1700CST window. Representative snowfall amounts were 1.2 inches of snow at Vermillion and near Elk Point, 1.6 inches at Sioux Falls, 3.0 inches near Brookings, 4.5 inches near Bruce and Huron, 2.0 inches at Mitchell, 4.0 inches at Platte and 2 miles northeast of Academy, 5.0 inches at White Lake, and 5.5 inches 8 miles south southwest of Herrick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance rotating around a large Canadian upper vortex brought a period of enhanced frontogenetic forcing through the Missouri River valley and eastward across northwest Iowa and far southern Minnesota.  Snowfall averaged from 2 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota. While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across southeast South Dakota, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts were mainly 1 to 2 inches across far east and southeast South Dakota, and 3 to 5 inches from south central South Dakota into east central South Dakota. As a result of the snowfall rate, roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across southeast South Dakota, with four to six hours of moderate snowfall during the 0800CST to 1700CST window. Representative snowfall amounts were 1.2 inches of snow at Vermillion and near Elk Point, 1.6 inches at Sioux Falls, 3.0 inches near Brookings, 4.5 inches near Bruce and Huron, 2.0 inches at Mitchell, 4.0 inches at Platte and 2 miles northeast of Academy, 5.0 inches at White Lake, and 5.5 inches 8 miles south southwest of Herrick.

The melt water from the deep mid-April snow pack resulted in widespread flooding across Deuel county from the 21st to the 28th. Many roads and cropland were flooded across the region.
The prolonged severe drought across western Corson and extreme northwest Dewey counties finally came to an end in the middle of April.
The prolonged severe drought across western Corson and extreme northwest Dewey counties finally came to an end in the middle of April.

An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6. 500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened. As a result, winds increased to a gusty 35 to 45 mph and produced brief periods of near whiteout conditions. The system was dynamic enough to produce a few thunderstorms early on March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous winter weather evolved during the morning of March 5, as rain changed to light to moderate snowfall and was accompanied by winds gusting as high as 43 mph at Sioux Falls, 37 mph at Madison and 35 mph at Brookings. There were brief blizzard or near blizzard conditions during the midday hours around Sioux Falls, but not persistent enough to be defined as a blizzard. Total snowfall was generally 3 to 6 inches, including 4.2 inches at Brookings. Flandreau and Sioux Falls, and 3.6 inches 2 miles north of Chester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall brought middle portions of the Big Sioux River above flood stage in early March. Crests were at minor flood stage near Hawarden (HWDI4) and Akron (AKRI4), impacting only lowland agricultural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along middle portions of the Big Sioux River. The river reached a crest of 0.1 feet above flood stage near Akron (gage site AKRI4) on March 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt along with periods of rainfall and some minor ice jams through mid March caused minor flooding along portions of the Vermillion River. Impacts were found generally in lowland agricultural locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt and some ice jamming caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along portions of the Vermillion River. The river reached an estimated crest of 0.6 feet above flood stage near Wakonda (gage site WKAS2) on March 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough again progressed through the Rockies and evolved to a compact closed mid-level circulation drifting across eastern Nebraska and Iowa on April 18. Weak instability resulted in some scattered thunder with sleet and snow from the lower Missouri River valley through northwest Iowa. Marginally warmer temperatures aloft allowed for some light mixed precipitation early on April 18, but a quick transition to snow occurred in the pre-dawn hours, leaving behind between 5 and 11 inches snowfall across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of mixed precipitation, snow became dominant after 0200CST. Visibility dropped to a half mile or less at times between 0300-0900CST. Snowfall totaled 8.4 inches 10 miles west of Dimock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very warm day, a cold front pushing across southeast South Dakota during the evening produced scattered thunderstorms, with one severe storm toward south central South Dakota.
Much of the James River basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the weeks leading up to the flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall produced minor flooding which impacted lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 0.2 feet above flood stage on the James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) on April 30.  The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Snow and strong winds developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to Interstate 29 in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of light freezing rain during the morning of December 1, light to occasionally moderate snowfall produced an accumulation of 3.2 inches eight miles south-southwest of Chamberlain and 3.0 inches five miles south of Chamberlain. Hazardous travel resulted as winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce visibility of a mile or less between 1600-1900CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of cold air following a late December winter storm brought wind chills from 20 below to 30 below zero during the evening of December 28 through early morning of December 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest winds at times from 5 to 12 mph combined with air temperatures of 10 below to 18 below zero to produce wind chills around -20 to -30 during the evening to early morning hours. Coldest wind chills included Flandreau (-29), Huron (-27), Brookings (-26), Madison (-26) and White Lake (-25).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme wind chills which began on December 30th, 2017 across central and northeast South Dakota continued into January 1st. Wind chills of 35 to near 55 degrees below zero occurred off and on during this time.  Record lows set on the morning of January 1st were in the 30s below zero with even some 40s below zero.  Some of the record lows on January 1st include -30 degrees at Mobridge, -32 degrees at Aberdeen and Timber Lake, -35 degrees at Kennebec, and -44 degrees 17 miles west southwest of Fort Pierre.  Temperatures did not respond well for daytime highs on January 1st as several record low highs in the single digits below zero occurred. Some of the most bitter wind chills on the 1st include -45 degrees at Mobridge and Eureka, -49 degrees at Aberdeen, -50 degrees at Summit, and -55 degrees at Shambo Ranch in Corson county. See December 2017 storm data for the beginning of this event.

Extreme wind chills which began on December 30th, 2017 across central and northeast South Dakota continued into January 1st. Wind chills of 35 to near 55 degrees below zero occurred off and on during this time.  Record lows set on the morning of January 1st were in the 30s below zero with even some 40s below zero.  Some of the record lows on January 1st include -30 degrees at Mobridge, -32 degrees at Aberdeen and Timber Lake, -35 degrees at Kennebec, and -44 degrees 17 miles west southwest of Fort Pierre.  Temperatures did not respond well for daytime highs on January 1st as several record low highs in the single digits below zero occurred. Some of the most bitter wind chills on the 1st include -45 degrees at Mobridge and Eureka, -49 degrees at Aberdeen, -50 degrees at Summit, and -55 degrees at Shambo Ranch in Corson county. See December 2017 storm data for the beginning of this event.

A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains. As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm. Snow developed later on January 21, and was followed by a rapid increase of northwest winds to a gusty 30 to 45 mph early on January 22. Widespread visibility of a mile or less at time occurred with falling snow, heavy at times, which continued into early afternoon. Snowfall totaled to 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, heavy at times, developed early on January 21, with an increase in wind to a gusty 30 to 40 mph by early on January 22 producing areas of blowing snow.  Travel become quite challenging with visibilities often at a mile or less through the morning of January 22. Total snowfall tallied generally 4 to 7 inches, but measured as high as 8.5 inches 2 miles northeast of Burke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains. As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm. Snow developed on January 21, and was followed by a rapid increase of northwest winds to a gusty 30 to 40 mph early on January 22. Visibility at times dipped to around a mile with falling and/or blowing snow, creating difficult travel conditions. Snowfall totals varied from around an inch or less toward Interstate 29, to 2 to 5 inches west of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early on January 21 and continued through early January 22 west of the James River, while snow did not develop until late on January 21 east of the James River. Winds increased overnight into the morning hours of the 22nd, gusting from 30 to 40 mph and creating areas of blowing snow along Interstate 90.  Peak winds on January 22 reached 31 mph at White Lake around 0700CST, 39 mph at Mitchell around 0800CST, 44 mph at Sioux Falls around 0800CST and 36 mph at Brookings around 1100CST. Snowfall totalled 5.0 inches at Wessington Springs, 4.1 inches at Chamberlain, 0.5 inch at Mitchell, 2.5 inches at Alexandria, 1.2 inches at Flandreau and 2.7 inches in Sioux Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2.  The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning.  Heavy snow and wind developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to Interstate 29 in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain which produced a glaze of light icing during the early morning of December 1, heavy snow occurred for numerous hours. Accumulations over  less than a 24-hour period totaled 7.5 inches 1 mile south of Armour. Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce several hours of visibility of a half mile or less along with treacherous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough pushing through the region failed to phase a northern and southern stream component, resulting in limited and fairly light snowfall, but a period of strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of snowfall moved across eastern South Dakota and weakened during the early hours of January 11. Greatest snowfall from 1 to 3 inches occurred south of Interstate 90 from the lower James River valley toward Sioux Falls, with the highest reported amounts reported at Menno (2.5 inches), Marion (2.4 inches) and 3.9 SW Sioux Falls (2.3 inches). Blustery northwest winds occurred with the snowfall and well through January 11, with gusts to 45 mph reported at Yankton and Madison, 43 mph at Sioux Falls, and 41 mph at Brookings. Visibility dropped briefly to a half mile to mile, creating difficult travel conditions near and south of Interstate 90 and west of Interstate 29.

Some of the record lows on January 1st include -30 degrees at Mobridge, -32 degrees at Aberdeen and Timber Lake, -35 degrees at Kennebec, and -44 degrees 17 miles west southwest of Fort Pierre.  Temperatures did not respond well for daytime highs on January 1st as several record low highs in the single digits below zero occurred. Some of the most bitter wind chills on the 1st include -45 degrees at Mobridge and Eureka, -49 degrees at Aberdeen, -50 degrees at Summit, and -55 degrees at Shambo Ranch in Corson county. See December 2017 storm data for the beginning of this event.

A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2.  The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning.  Heavy snow and wind developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to Interstate 29 in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain which produced a glaze of light icing during the early morning of December 1, heavy snow occurred for numerous hours. Accumulations over less than a 24-hour period totaled 8.0 inches six miles south of Alcester and 6.8 inches six miles north of Elk Point. Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce several hours of visibility of a half mile or less along with treacherous travel. Numerous cars slid into the ditch along Interstate 29, but no significant damage was reported.

A quick moving clipper system deepened rapidly and carved out a closed upper level low across the western Great Lakes as it dropped southeast from the Canadian prairie provinces on the evening of January 14.  The system produced a dusting of less than an inch snowfall into January 15, as well as a brutal rush of arctic air into the region with winds gusting up to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph along with temperatures falling to subzero levels produced dangerous to life threatening wind chills from -35 to -40. Wind chills reached -36 at Brookings, Flandreau, and De Smet.  Wind chills colder than -20 persisted consistently through 1700CST January 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold air remained across the area into the beginning of the new year. Lows on New Year's Day morning ranged from 15 below to 35 below zero across much of the area, with some locations across northwestern South Dakota dropping to 35 below to 45 below. Wind chills were as low as 35 below to 50 below on the plains. Very cold conditions continued into the morning of January 2 across northwestern and west central South Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of cold air following a late December winter storm brought wind chills from 20 below to 30 below zero during the evening of December 28 through early morning of December 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest winds at times from 5 to 12 mph combined with air temperatures of 10 below to 18 below zero to produce wind chills around -20 to -30 during the evening to early morning hours. Coldest wind chills included Flandreau (-29), Huron (-27), Brookings (-26), Madison (-26) and White Lake (-25).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills from -35 to -45 continued through the morning hours across southeast South Dakota as a strong arctic surface ridge continued to slide into the Northern Plains. This continued the extreme cold which initiated during late December 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest winds at times from 7 to 12 mph combined with air temperatures of 20 below to 33 below zero to produce bitterly cold wind chills around -35 to -45 through the morning hours of New Year's Day.  Many locations with over a century of records reached record low air temperatures including Menno and 6 SE of Centerville (-33), Gregory (-31), 2 NE of Academy and 4 NE of Forestburg (-30), Alexandria (-29), White Lake and 2 SE Vermillion (-28), De Smet (-27) and Wessington Springs (-26). As southerly return flow developed behind the retreating ridge, not as extreme wind chills of -20 to -30 occurred from the evening of January 1st into mid morning of January 2nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to 25 to 35 mph along with temperatures falling to subzero levels produced dangerous wind chills from -25 to -35. A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Snow and strong winds developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to Interstate 29 in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of light freezing rain during the morning of December 1, light to occasionally moderate snowfall produced an accumulation of 3.0 inches at Bridgewater. Hazardous travel resulted as winds gusted to 35 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce visibility of a mile or less between 1400-1700CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough pushing through the region failed to phase a northern and southern stream component, resulting in limited and fairly light snowfall, but a period of strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of snowfall moved across areas from the James Valley westward during the evening of January 10 and very early on January 11. Greatest snowfall from 2 to 4 inches occurred south of Interstate 90, with the highest reported amounts reported 4.6 miles W Armour (4.0 inches) and 8.2 miles SSW of Herrick (3.5 inches). Blustery northwest winds occurred with snowfall and well into January 11, with gusts to 45 mph reported at Mitchell, 43 mph at Chamberlain and Huron, and 47 mph at Gregory. Visibilities dropped to a half mile to mile for a brief time, creating difficult travel conditions.

Many locations with over a century of records reached record low air temperatures including Menno and 6 SE of Centerville (-33), Gregory (-31), 2 NE of Academy and 4 NE of Forestburg (-30), Alexandria (-29), White Lake and 2 SE Vermillion (-28), De Smet (-27) and Wessington Springs (-26). As southerly return flow developed behind the retreating ridge, not as extreme wind chills of -20 to -30 occurred from the evening of January 1st into mid morning of January 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough pushing through the region failed to phase a northern and southern stream component, resulting in limited and fairly light snowfall, but a period of strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of snowfall moved across eastern South Dakota and weakened during the early hours of January 11. Greatest snowfall from 1 to 3 inches occurred south of Interstate 90 from the lower James River valley toward Sioux Falls, with the highest reported amounts reported at Menno (2.5 inches), Marion (2.4 inches) and 3.9 SW Sioux Falls (2.3 inches). Blustery northwest winds occurred with the snowfall and well through January 11, with gusts to 45 mph reported at Yankton and Madison, 43 mph at Sioux Falls, and 41 mph at Brookings. Visibility dropped briefly to a half mile to mile, creating difficult travel conditions near and south of Interstate 90 and west of Interstate 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air rushed southward into the region behind light snow producing system on the evening of January 11 and through the morning of January 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds at 10 to 15 mph combined with subzero temperatures to produce wind chills from 20 below to 30 below zero. Minimum wind chill values of -28 at Brookings, Madison, and Flandreau, -24 at Huron and Sioux Falls, -22 at White Lake and -21 at Mitchell were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Snow and strong winds developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to Interstate 29 in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of light freezing rain during the morning of December 1, light to occasionally moderate snowfall produced an accumulation of 3.0 inches at Howard. Hazardous travel resulted as winds gusted to 35 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce visibility of a mile or less between 1400-1700CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains.  As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm.  Initially warm enough for rain, colder air rushed in as northwest winds rapidly increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph and brought a rapid change to snow late on January 21 and early January 22. Widespread visibility of a quarter mile or less with snow, heavy at times, continued through mid afternoon.  Snow amassed to 8 to 13 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 0000CST January 22, with northwest winds increasing during the early morning to a gusty 25 to 40 mph. Frequent wind gusts along with heavy falling snow produced near zero visibility by daybreak, and continued into mid afternoon. Poor road conditions led several vehicles to slide into the ditch or become stuck, as well as a few jackknifed trucks. State officials issued a no travel advisory around 0700CST, which was enhanced when Interstate 29 was closed from Tea, South Dakota to the Iowa border just after noon. Winds peaked at 40 mph at 1100CST.  Conditions gradually improved toward 1600CST, and eventually Interstate 29 was opened again around 2130CST. Total snowfall was measured at 10.4 inches at Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains.  As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm.  Initially warm enough for rain, colder air rushed in as northwest winds rapidly increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph and brought a rapid change to snow late on January 21 and early January 22. Widespread visibility of a quarter mile or less with snow, heavy at times, continued through mid afternoon.  Snow amassed to 8 to 13 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 0000CST January 22, with northwest winds increasing during the early morning to a gusty 35 to 45 mph. Frequent wind gusts along with heavy falling snow produced near zero visibility before daybreak, which continued into mid afternoon. Poor road conditions led several vehicles to slide into the ditch or become stuck, as well as a few jackknifed trucks. State officials issued a no travel advisory around 0700CST, which was enhanced when Interstate 29 was closed from Tea, South Dakota to the Iowa border just after noon. Winds peaked at 40 mph at 1100CST.  Conditions gradually improved toward 1800CST, and eventually Interstate 29 was opened again around 2130CST. Total snowfall was measured at 12.0 inches at Elk Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second long track severe thunderstorm moved out of western South Dakota and across central South Dakota through the evening hours. This storm produced large hail up to 2 inches along with winds to nearly 90 mph as it moved across the region. A nearly continuous path of destruction occurred from northwest Stanley county east through central Sully county and across northern Hyde county. The path was nearly 90 miles across this area with a path width varying from 2 to 5 miles. Tens of thousands of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm with some farmers losing up to 15,000 acres alone.||The hail and wind storm also wreaked havoc on vehicles, buildings, and windows. Campers were toppled, grain bins and building siding were damaged with some roofs being blown off some structures.  Also, some wildlife, including birds and deer, were killed from the storm. Thirty-two power poles were downed west of Onida along with three transmission poles broken or leaning, leaving many without electricity through the night. Power lines and poles on the roads halted travel for some areas. This storm crossed some of the same areas in Sully county that were hit by destructive hail on June 27th. This hail and wind storm had began in Wyoming and traveled over 420 miles before it ended in eastern South Dakota.

Severe thunderstorms developed along a stationary front and brought large hail, damaging winds, flooding, along with a few tornadoes to parts of central and northeast South Dakota. Two tornadoes and large hail caused the most damage near Burdette in Hand county.

Upper level trough pushing through the region failed to phase a northern and southern stream component, resulting in limited and fairly light snowfall, but a period of strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of snowfall moved across eastern South Dakota and weakened during the early hours of January 11. Greatest snowfall from 1 to 3 inches occurred south of Interstate 90 from the lower James River valley toward Sioux Falls, with the highest reported amounts reported at Menno (2.5 inches), Marion (2.4 inches) and 3.9 SW Sioux Falls (2.3 inches). Blustery northwest winds occurred with the snowfall and well through January 11, with gusts to 45 mph reported at Yankton and Madison, 43 mph at Sioux Falls, and 41 mph at Brookings. Visibility dropped briefly to a half mile to mile, creating difficult travel conditions near and south of Interstate 90 and west of Interstate 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system moved across the Northern and Central Plains, bringing snow to much of western and south central South Dakota. Much of the area received six to 12 inches of snow, with the exception being far northwestern South Dakota, which saw lighter amounts. Winds were on the lighter side with the system, so blowing and drifting snow was a minor issue.

A couple upper level low pressure troughs brought a couple rounds of heavy snow to parts of central and north central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches occurred. The first round brought 2 to 4 inches while the second round brought 4 to 7  inches. Some of the heaviest snowfall amounts include, 8 inches at Onida, Fort Pierre, and Highmore, 9 inches at Miller, and 11 inches south of Harrold in Hughes county.

A strong storm system brought snow to much of far western South Dakota during the overnight and morning hours. The heaviest snow fell across the northern and central Black Hills, where precipitation changed over to snowfall earlier. Snowfall amounts of four to eight inches fell across much of these areas. Elsewhere, snowfall totals of two to six inches were reported across portions of far western South Dakota.

Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota. While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across southeast South Dakota, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts were mainly 1 to 2 inches across far east and southeast South Dakota, and 3 to 5 inches from south central South Dakota into east central South Dakota. As a result of the snowfall rate, roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across southeast South Dakota, with four to six hours of moderate snowfall during the 0800CST to 1700CST window. Representative snowfall amounts were 1.2 inches of snow at Vermillion and near Elk Point, 1.6 inches at Sioux Falls, 3.0 inches near Brookings, 4.5 inches near Bruce and Huron, 2.0 inches at Mitchell, 4.0 inches at Platte and 2 miles northeast of Academy, 5.0 inches at White Lake, and 5.5 inches 8 miles south southwest of Herrick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a band of heavy snow to portions of northwest South Dakota. Snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell from western and southern portions of Harding County to northern Butte, northern Meade, and Ziebach counties. A narrow band of heavy snow also developed over the northeastern foothills of the Black Hills, from Whitewood to Piedmont.

A disturbance rotating around a large Canadian upper vortex brought a period of enhanced frontogenetic forcing through the Missouri River valley and eastward across northwest Iowa and far southern Minnesota.  Snowfall averaged from 2 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow over a period of several hours brought from 2 to 5 inches of snowfall across portions of southeast South Dakota south of Interstate 90. Higher totals were measured near Bonesteel (4.4 inches), Tyndall (3.7 inches), Gregory (3.5 inches), Menno (3.0 inches), Platte (2.5 inches), near Chamberlain (1.9 inches) and White Lake (1.5 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27.  A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN.  This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28.  A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN.||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing briefly to light freezing rain between 1800-2300CST.  Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times.  Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 30 to 40 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28.  Snowfall accumulated to 10.0 inches 2 miles south of Astoria, 8.7 inches 5 miles west of Bruce, and 7.0 to 8.0 inches around Brookings with a peak wind gust of 41 mph. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure area brought scattered showers and thunderstorms along with heavy snow to much of central and north central South Dakota from the 5th to the 6th. There were some instances of thundersnow reported. The scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across the region during the early morning hours of the 5th while widespread heavy snow and very strong northwest winds developed by mid morning on the 5th. Northwest winds gusting to 40 to 60 mph brought near whiteout conditions.  In fact, Pierre had a visibility of less than quarter mile from 8 am CST to 11 pm CST on the 5th. The blizzard and heavy snow resulted in closed businesses, schools, government offices, nearly impossible travel with several accidents reported, along with closed highways and Insterstate-90.  Many activities and events were also postponed or cancelled.||Some snowfall amounts from across the region include, 3 inches at Kennebec and Murdo; 6 inches at Gann Valley; 7 inches at Timber Lake and Pollock; 8 inches at Miller, Mobridge, and Gettysburg; 9 inches at Pierre and southwest of Harrold; 10 inches northeast of Java; and 12 inches at Herreid. ||Due to the track of the surface low pressure area, the eastern part of our region experienced mainly very heavy snow with much less wind. Therefore, refer to the winter storm entry for March 5th and 6th for more information.

The scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across the region during the early morning hours of the 5th while widespread heavy snow and very strong northwest winds developed by mid morning on the 5th. Northwest winds gusting to 40 to 60 mph brought near whiteout conditions.  In fact, Pierre had a visibility of less than quarter mile from 8 am CST to 11 pm CST on the 5th. The blizzard and heavy snow resulted in closed businesses, schools, government offices, nearly impossible travel with several accidents reported, along with closed highways and Insterstate-90.  Many activities and events were also postponed or cancelled.||Some snowfall amounts from across the region include, 3 inches at Kennebec and Murdo; 6 inches at Gann Valley; 7 inches at Timber Lake and Pollock; 8 inches at Miller, Mobridge, and Gettysburg; 9 inches at Pierre and southwest of Harrold; 10 inches northeast of Java; and 12 inches at Herreid. ||Due to the track of the surface low pressure area, the eastern part of our region experienced mainly very heavy snow with much less wind. Therefore, refer to the winter storm entry for March 5th and 6th for more information.

An intense surface low pressure area brought scattered showers and thunderstorms along with heavy snow to much of north central and northeast South Dakota from the 5th to the 6th.  The scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across the region during the early morning hours of the 5th while heavy snow developed from the mid morning to the early afternoon.  There were several reports of thundersnow across the region. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to as much as 18 inches before it ended on the 6th. The very heavy snow resulted in closed businesses, schools, government offices, difficult travel conditions with several accidents reported, along with closed highways and Insterstate-29.  Many activities and events were also postponed or cancelled.||Some snowfall amounts from across the region include, 7 inches at Britton; 8 inches at Faulkton, Eureka, Bowdle, Watertown, and Seneca; 9 inches at Aberdeen and Tulare; 10 inches at Garden City; 11 inches at Leola, Summit, and Sisseton. Locations with a foot or more include, 12 inches at Milbank and Clear Lake; 13 inches at La Bolt and Craven Corner; 14 inches at Wilmot; 15 inches near Twin Brooks; 16 inches at Frederick; and 18 inches at Gary, Spring Creek Colony, and Elm Lake. ||Due to the track of the surface low pressure area, the western part of our region experienced heavy snow and very strong northwest winds bringing blizzard conditions. Therefore, refer to the blizzard entry for March 5th and 6th for more information.

A strong low pressure system passed across the Northern Plains, bringing very gusty winds and some snow to the far western South Dakota plains. Snowfall amounts were generally less than an inch across these areas. However, strong northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph and gusts as high as 70 mph affected the area during the daytime hours.

A strong low pressure system brought snow and very gusty winds from northwestern to south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts were only in the one to three inch range across much of the area, but wind gusts as high as 65 mph produced blizzard conditions, with near zero visibility at times. The worst conditions were across central South Dakota.

A storm system brought a band of heavy snow to portions of northwest South Dakota. Snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell from western and southern portions of Harding County to northern Butte, northern Meade, and Ziebach counties. A narrow band of heavy snow also developed over the northeastern foothills of the Black Hills, from Whitewood to Piedmont.

A storm system brought a band of heavy snow to portions of northwest South Dakota. Snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell from western and southern portions of Harding County to northern Butte, northern Meade, and Ziebach counties. A narrow band of heavy snow also developed over the northeastern foothills of the Black Hills, from Whitewood to Piedmont.

The Big Sioux River near Castlewood went above flood stage for short time period at the end of March with minimal affects. The river at Castlewood rose above the flood stage of 9 feet to 9.68 feet from the 28th to the 29th.

An upper level system moved across the Northern and Central Plains, bringing snow to much of western and south central South Dakota. Much of the area received six to 12 inches of snow, with the exception being far northwestern South Dakota, which saw lighter amounts. Winds were on the lighter side with the system, so blowing and drifting snow was a minor issue.

A strong low pressure system brought snow and very gusty winds from northwestern to south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts were only in the one to three inch range across much of the area, but wind gusts as high as 65 mph produced blizzard conditions, with near zero visibility at times. The worst conditions were across central South Dakota.

A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27.  A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN.  This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28.  A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN.||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing briefly to light freezing rain or rain from 1200-0000CST.  Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times.  Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 35 to 45 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28.  Snowfall accumulated to 8.0 inches 5 miles northeast of Salem and 6.0 inches at Montrose, with a peak wind gust of 43 mph at Sioux Falls and 48 mph at Madison. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota. While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across southeast South Dakota, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts were mainly 1 to 2 inches across far east and southeast South Dakota, and 3 to 5 inches from south central South Dakota into east central South Dakota. As a result of the snowfall rate, roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across southeast South Dakota, with four to six hours of moderate snowfall during the 0800CST to 1700CST window. Representative snowfall amounts were 1.2 inches of snow at Vermillion and near Elk Point, 1.6 inches at Sioux Falls, 3.0 inches near Brookings, 4.5 inches near Bruce and Huron, 2.0 inches at Mitchell, 4.0 inches at Platte and 2 miles northeast of Academy, 5.0 inches at White Lake, and 5.5 inches 8 miles south southwest of Herrick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second disturbance in three days traversed the deep western trough, and the associated jet streak produced another area of snowfall across southeast South Dakota. A narrow band of heavier snowfall produced accumulations from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, occasionally moderate to heavy from 1400CST through 1800CST, produced accumulations of 8.0 inches near Parkston and 4.0 inches at Menno.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, briefly moderate from 1400CST to 1700CST, totaled 2.1 inches near Chamberlain and 2.0 inches 5 miles south of Chamberlain.

Some of the heaviest snowfall amounts include, 8 inches at Onida, Fort Pierre, and Highmore, 9 inches at Miller, and 11 inches south of Harrold in Hughes county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most intense storm of the month, in fact many months, wrapped up across the central Plains on April 13, spreading a mix of wintry precipitation across the area, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms.  Not included in this episode was the spotty severe hail and wind which occurred in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota prior to the onset of snowfall during the morning and afternoon of April 13.|Eventually, influx of colder air would change all precipitation over to snow,  and ushered in a record-breaking snowfall of 8 to 16 inches (some locations over 20 inches) and fierce winds gusting 50 to 60 mph to southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa and extreme northeast Nebraska. Snow drifts over 6 feet were reported in many locations.  Extremely low visibility persisted for almost a day for most locations, making travel very difficult if not impossible. ||One person was killed in Rock County when the snowmobile he was riding collided with an interstate guard rail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life threatening conditions developed during the evening hours of April 13, as the earlier mix of rain, sleet and snow changed to all snow, and brutal winds (gusting over 40 mph) whipped visibility to less than a quarter mile at times through the night and following day of April 14. State offices were closed and schools cancelled on April 13. Travel was not recommended for much of the two day period, if not impossible.  A storm total snowfall of 6.6 inches was measured at Tyndall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet pushing across the Rockies resulted in development of low pressure across southeast Colorado late on April 2, which intensified rapidly moving across Kansas and into Illinois on April 3. A broad swath of snow and gusty winds developed north of the low track across the area, with heaviest snowfall amounts in excess of 6 inches generally corresponding to highest terrain areas in south central and southeast South Dakota, northern portions of northwest Iowa and areas of southwest Minnesota. As unseasonably cold high pressure spread in behind the system on the morning of April 4, a few low temperature records were broken by as much as 5 to 15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of more intense snowfall developed in the morning hours of April 3, continuing at times into early afternoon. Winds shifted northwest and increased substantially with gusts of 30 to 40 mph at times through the late morning and afternoon, lowering visibility under a mile at times 0800-1300CST. Snowfall totaled 1.3 inches at Mitchell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another amplifying wave in rapid west-northwest flow aloft brought a quick shot of snowfall to the region. With lapse rates slightly unstable on the warm side of the baroclinic zone, strong frontogenetic forcing resulted in a narrow band and brief of very intense snowfall which resulting in less than a half mile visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall developed quickly in the morning and continued briefly heavy at times into early afternoon. Southeast wind gusted to 30-35 mph and resulted in periods of visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile at times. Snowfall reached 5.6 inches at 5 miles southeast of Sioux Falls, 4.8 inches at the NWS Office in Sioux Falls and 4.5 inches near Hartford and at Wall Lake.  The snowfall which reached around 1-2 inches per hour at times between 1000-1400CST.  At the peak around 1040CST, Allegiant Air Flight 456 attempted to land and skidded 20 feet off the end of the runway. There were no injuries or significant damage, but passengers were forced to remain on the plane until around 1300CST.

The most intense storm of the month, in fact many months, wrapped up across the central Plains on April 13, spreading a mix of wintry precipitation across the area, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms.  Not included in this episode was the spotty severe hail and wind which occurred in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota prior to the onset of snowfall during the morning and afternoon of April 13.|Eventually, influx of colder air would change all precipitation over to snow,  and ushered in a record-breaking snowfall of 8 to 16 inches (some locations over 20 inches) and fierce winds gusting 50 to 60 mph to southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa and extreme northeast Nebraska. Snow drifts over 6 feet were reported in many locations.  Extremely low visibility persisted for almost a day for most locations, making travel very difficult if not impossible. ||One person was killed in Rock County when the snowmobile he was riding collided with an interstate guard rail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life threatening conditions developed during the late afternoon of April 13, as the earlier mix of rain, sleet and snow changed to all snow, and brutal winds (gusting as high as 60 mph at Mitchell and 56 mph at Huron) whipped visibility to less than a quarter mile at times through the morning of April 14. State offices were closed and schools cancelled on April 13. Travel was not recommended for much of the two day period, if not impossible.  A storm total snowfall of 18.0 inches was measured 3 miles northeast of Forestburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another amplifying wave in rapid west-northwest flow aloft brought a quick shot of snowfall to the region. With lapse rates slightly unstable on the warm side of the baroclinic zone, strong frontogenetic forcing resulted in a narrow band and brief of very intense snowfall which resulting in less than a half mile visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall developed quickly in the morning and remained heavy at times into early afternoon. Southeast wind gusted to 30-35 mph and resulted in periods of visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile at times. Snowfall reached 9.5 inches 3 miles west southwest of Brookings and 8.3 inches at Brookings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet pushing across the Rockies resulted in development of low pressure across southeast Colorado late on April 2, which intensified rapidly moving across Kansas and into Illinois on April 3. As unseasonably cold high pressure spread in behind the system on the morning of April 4, a few low temperature records were broken by as much as 5 to 15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of more intense snowfall developed in the early morning hours of April 3, continuing at times into early afternoon. Winds shifted northwest and increased substantially with gusts of 30 to 40 mph at times through the late morning and afternoon, lowering visibility under a mile at times 0800-1300CST. Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches 4 miles west of Armour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure trough wrapped in a mixed bag of precipitation across central South Dakota during the morning and afternoon hours. The warmer air aloft riding over the near freezing to subfreezing temperatures at the surface brought widespread rain, freezing rain, and sleet resulting in significant ice accumulations. Due to the near to just below freezing temperatures, most of the ice accumulations occurred on trees, power lines, and structures with the roads surfaces mainly becoming slushy. There were a few reports of power outages. ||Total precipitation amounts ranged from a few tenths of an inch to nearly an inch and a quarter across the region. Of this precipitation, ice accumulations of a quarter to a half inch along with up to three tenths of an inch of sleet occurred along with one location receiving 10 inches of snow. The precipitation changed over to all snow later in the afternoon as the colder air took over the region with all locations receiving from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall before it all ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tenth of an inch of ice accumulation along with 10 inches of snow occurred near Sansarc.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet pushing across the Rockies resulted in development of low pressure across southeast Colorado late on April 2, which intensified rapidly moving across Kansas and into Illinois on April 3. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another amplifying wave in rapid west-northwest flow aloft brought a quick shot of snowfall to the region. Runoff from snow melt in late March worked through the upper and middle portions of the Big Sioux River, resulting in minor to moderate flooding of mainly agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from March 2018, the river crested at minor flood level near Brookings (gage site BRKS2) 1.2 feet above flood stage on April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Big Sioux basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the more significant flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14. Much of the river remained in flood at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall produced major flooding which produced significant impact to lowland agricultural areas. Several county roads were inundated with water as river levels reached 3.3 feet above flood stage near Brookings (gage site BRKS2) on April 24. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance brought snowfall to much of western South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell from northwestern South Dakota into the northern Black Hills, where four to eight inches were reported. Amounts to ten inches were noted in the Spearfish area. Accumulations of two to five inches were reported across west central South Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm brought rain and snow to the region. Rain changed to snow across far western South Dakota during the early morning and across the rest of western into south central South Dakota during the late morning and early afternoon. Snow was heavy at times across the South Dakota plains, especially southeast of the Black Hills into south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from four to eight inches across far western South Dakota to 12 to 20 inches over south central South Dakota. North to northwest winds gusting to 50 mph produced blizzard conditions at times across the west central and south central South Dakota plains. Interstate 90 was closed east of Rapid City for about 24 hours.

Runoff from melting snow across southeastern Montana caused minor flooding along the Little Missouri River upstream and downstream of Camp Crook. Minor flooding of pasture land occurred along the river for a couple of days, with a peak flow of around 4800 cubic feet per second.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the Little Missouri River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains and interacted with a nearly stationary region of baroclinicity during the following week.  High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 2 to 6 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 2 to 6 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20 and 25. Specific weekly totals including 2.05 at Mitchell, 4.74 at Menno, 4.80 at Parkston, 5.36 11 miles south of Emery and 6.83 near Yankton. Significant amounts of crop and pasture land were inundated as the river crested near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) 0.4 feet above flood stage on June 27. The river dropped below flood stage on June 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed on the nose of the low-level jet across central South Dakota and progressed east and southeast during the late overnight and early morning hours.  Before storms outraced the greater elevated instability axis and weakened, a few storms made use of the effective shear of 30-35 knots and produced severe hail and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was accompanied by winds of greater than 50 mph.

After a line of strong convection pushed across the area during the late evening and early overnight, a stratiform area of precipitation generated an area of strong wind gusts between 0030 and 0300CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds developed within a stratiform precipitation area well behind convection which moved across the mid-James River valley late evening.  Winds were sustained between 25 and 35 mph between 0000 and 0200CST, with the peak gust occurring at 0111CST.  Visibility during this time was 3 miles or less due to blowing dust, and numerous large tree limbs were broken from trees around Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains and interacted with a nearly stationary region of baroclinicity during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 5 to 9 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from the previous day caused significant ponding of water and drastic increases in small stream levels. County roads were closed as waters from Frog Creek and Turkey Ridge Creek inundated roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains and interacted with a nearly stationary region of baroclinicity during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 5 to 9 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 5 to 9 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20 and 25. Specific weekly totals including 6.08 at Montrose, 7.59 near Hurley and 8.57 at Marion. Major flooding occurred with significant amounts of crop and pasture land as well as most county roads were inundated as the river crested near Parker (gage site PKRS2) 2.5 feet above flood stage on June 21. The river dropped below flood stage on June 23, but returned above flood stage with moderate flooding again on June 26 before falling back below flood stage on June 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm brought rain and snow to the region. Rain changed to snow across far western South Dakota during the early morning and across the rest of western into south central South Dakota during the late morning and early afternoon. Snow was heavy at times across the South Dakota plains, especially southeast of the Black Hills into south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from four to eight inches across far western South Dakota to 12 to 20 inches over south central South Dakota. North to northwest winds gusting to 50 mph produced blizzard conditions at times across the west central and south central South Dakota plains. Interstate 90 was closed east of Rapid City for about 24 hours.

A tornadic thunderstorm tracked east-northeast from Montana across Harding County during the evening. The storm produced several tornadoes, including four in Harding County. The largest of the tornadoes was rated EF-3 and caused significant damage at a couple of ranches.

A tornadic thunderstorm tracked east-northeast from Montana across Harding County during the evening. The storm produced several tornadoes, including four in Harding County. The largest of the tornadoes was rated EF-3 and caused significant damage at a couple of ranches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado started south of Capitol, Montana and crossed the state line into South Dakota approximately ten miles south-southwest of Camp Crook. The tornado produced considerable tree and structural damage west of South Camp Crook Road. It also lifted and destroyed some farm machinery, including a five-ton tractor that was found a few miles northwest in Montana. East of Camp Crook Road, a large farm outbuilding was destroyed and its foundation was extracted from the ground and broken apart. The tornado was rated an EF-3 with peak wind speeds estimated around 136 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornadic thunderstorm tracked east-northeast from Montana across Harding County during the evening. The storm produced several tornadoes, including four in Harding County. The largest of the tornadoes was rated EF-3 and caused significant damage at a couple of ranches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on eyewitness reports and video evidence, the tornado developed east of the first tornado near Johnson Draw and continued northeastward to near Waterhole Creek before dissipating. A home on Holcomb Place was damaged. Peak wind speeds were estimated near 120 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on eyewitness reports and video evidence, the tornado traveled north-northeastward then northwestward across open land. It caused no damage. Therefore, its EF rating could be determined.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornadic thunderstorm tracked east-northeast from Montana across Harding County during the evening. The storm produced several tornadoes, including four in Harding County. The largest of the tornadoes was rated EF-3 and caused significant damage at a couple of ranches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed just south of SD Highway 20 near mile marker 17, where it snapped power poles and trees near a house. A farm outbuilding was also severely damaged, with most of its exterior walls collapsed. Based on video evidence, the tornado then continued northward across rural areas northwest of Buffalo and crossed Clanton Road before dissipating. The tornado was rated an EF-2 with peak wind speeds estimated near 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6.  500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened.  As a result, winds increased to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and produced whiteout conditions at times.  The system was dynamic enough to produce thunderstorms with heavy rainfall early on March 5 toward far eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 44 mph at Parkston with snowfall of 6 inches at Parkston and 8 inches at Menno, producing whiteout conditions. SDDOT recommended no travel and suspended winter maintenance due to whiteout conditions from late on March 5 through early on March 6. Most schools were closed on March 5 and March 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas from far southern South Dakota and northeast Nebraska into adjacent northwest Iowa were on the northern fringes of an area of low pressure moving across the Central Plains.  Light ice accumulations were followed by light snowfall, resulting in slick roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel conditions producing several minor accidents resulted from a period of light freezing rain which brought ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch. Light snowfall followed with 1 to 3 inches of accumulation from near Interstate 90 southward and west of the James River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt along with periods of rainfall through mid March caused minor flooding along portions of the James River.  Impacts were found generally in lowland agricultural locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along Firesteel Creek. The river reached a crest of 3.0 feet above flood stage near Mount Vernon (gage site MTVS2) on March 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area moved southeast across the region and brought a band of heavy snow to parts of north central and northeast South Dakota. Six to 10 inches of snow fell from Mclaughlin to Clear Lake from the early morning to late evening. Some snowfall amounts include 6 inches at Selby, Mclaughlin, Roscoe, Faulkton, Mansfield, Webster, and Summit, 7 inches at Ipswich and Aberdeen, 8 inches at Clark and Hayti, 9 inches at Timber Lake, Watertown, and Castlewood, and 10 inches at Clear Lake.

The combination of dry conditions, freshly plowed fields and strong winds gusting up to 50 mph brought near zero visibility due to blowing dust across parts of Hand County. The dust storm conditions resulted in several car accidents in Hand county on a two mile stretch of a road near Miller. Vehicles either ran into each other or ran into the ditch. In one case, the dirt drifted around a vehicle trapping it on the side of the road. Some of the crashes sent people to the hospital with only minor injuries.  Fortunately, the dust storm was short-lived.
Winds gusted to 60 to 70 mph in the Sisseton Hills region during the early morning hours.

Snow melt water from the mid-April snowstorm brought the Big Sioux River in Codington county above flood stage on April 20th. The river rose above the 8 foot flood stage near Watertown to nearly 10 feet on April 22nd. The flooding continued into early May. Some agricultural land was flooded as a result.

A rare mid-April blizzard affected most of central and northeast South Dakota from April 13th into April 15th. Heavy snowfall along with high winds brought widespread blizzard conditions to the region. Snowfall amounts from 6 to as much as 20 inches along with wind gusts to over 60 mph brought hazardous, if not impossible travel. In fact, both Interstate-29 and Interstate-90 were closed due to the heavy snow and blizzard conditions.  Parts of both Interstates were re-opened on the 14th, while the rest opened on the morning of the 15th.||Dozens of schools were closed on the 13th along with many state government offices. Many activities and events were also postponed or cancelled. Despite the cancellations and delays, the South Dakota Highway patrol still responded to hundreds of calls for service to assist stranded motorists.  The wet conditions/mud, snow, and wind greatly affected cattle producers trying to protect newborn calves. As a result of the blizzard there were some calf losses across the region. The winds subsided on the 14th for many locations, leading to improved visibility, while periods of snow continued into the 15th across eastern South Dakota. ||Snowfall amounts include 6 inches at Onida and Watertown, 7 inches at Murdo, Highmore, and Pierre, 8 inches at Clark and 6 miles east of Hayes, 9 inches at Milbank and Orient, 10 inches at Seneca and 12 miles north of Highmore, and 11 inches at Castlewood and near Vivian.  Locations with a foot or more include 12 inches at Presho, 13 inches at Miller, 14 inches at Gann Valley, 17 inches at Kennebec, 18 inches at Clear Lake, and 20 inches near Gary.  The highest measured wind gust was 66 mph at Danforth in southern Hand county.

Heat index values reached 100-105 for several hours during the early to mid afternoon, before the outflow expanded to cool readings below advisory criteria.

During the morning of March 10, an approaching weak mid-level wave brought a brief pulse of warm advection. With cold temperatures at the surface and warmer temperatures aloft, a mix of precipitation types occurred during the morning and midday hours before changing over to light snow.  While most snowfall was less than an inch, a narrow band of one to four inches occurred from near De Smet to Chester and Flandreau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced ice accumulations of one to two tenths of an inch.

Thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary very late in the afternoon near the Nebraska border.  These storms were slow moving with weak flow aloft and anchored on this nearly stationary boundary through the evening hours.  Precipitable water approached 2 inches with up to 3500 J/kg MLCAPE south of the boundary.  A band of rainfall from 3 to 5 inches occurred during the evening hours from near Gregory to Parkston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water going over the road at intersection of State Highway 44 and 395th Street.  Heavy rain continues to fall at 1924CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across central and northeast South Dakota from the 2nd to the 3rd.  Some locations in north central South Dakota received heavy snow of 6 to 7 inches.||On the night of April 2nd, desperate measures had to be taken by law enforcement and staff of the Mobridge Regional Hospital. A patient needed O-negative blood, but the hospital had very little on hand. Due to wind, low visibility and an icy runway, no planes were able to land at the Mobridge Regional Airport. Law enforcement and hospital staff risked poor travel conditions and drove to surrounding communities to obtain 10 units of blood. Once conditions improved on April 3rd, a plane was able to land at the Mobridge Regional Airport and the patient was flown to Bismarck's CHI St. Alexius Health. The patient was able to make a full recovery.

Locations with a foot or more include 12 inches at Presho, 13 inches at Miller, 14 inches at Gann Valley, 17 inches at Kennebec, 18 inches at Clear Lake, and 20 inches near Gary.  The highest measured wind gust was 66 mph at Danforth in southern Hand county.

Locations with a foot or more include 12 inches at Presho, 13 inches at Miller, 14 inches at Gann Valley, 17 inches at Kennebec, 18 inches at Clear Lake, and 20 inches near Gary. Winds backing ahead of a strong upper level wave along with approach of a upper level jet streak increased late-day shear and supported development of an initially isolated severe thunderstorm across central South Dakota. This storm pressed southeast producing copious amounts of wind-driven hail, then was followed by a more linear severe system a few hours later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rainfall caused flooding of several county roads.

This storm pressed southeast producing copious amounts of wind-driven hail, then was followed by a more linear severe system a few hours later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars were stranded in foot deep waters on Broadway Street.  Report received via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas from far southern South Dakota and northeast Nebraska into adjacent northwest Iowa were on the northern fringes of an area of low pressure moving across the Central Plains.  Light ice accumulations were followed by light snowfall, resulting in slick roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel conditions producing several minor accidents resulted from a period of light freezing rain which brought ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch. Light snowfall followed with 1 to 3 inches of accumulation from near Interstate 90 southward and west of the James River.

A strong mid-level wave pushed southeast toward southwest Minnesota overnight and the early morning hours on July 19.  A large cluster of storms developed across eastern South Dakota after midnight, and with a high moisture content to the air mass, rainfall rates were quite high.  One storm on the southern edge of the complex intensified, with strong 0-3km shear with low-level jet contributing to a brief meso-vortex development as the storm moved into the southern side of Brookings, SD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Third Street in Brookings is under water due to torrential rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over U.S. Highway 14. Report received through social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed in a low-CAPE, low-shear environment during the afternoon.  A weak boundary concentrated low-level shear and 0-3km CAPE, which storms were able to use to spin up a couple brief tornadoes during the developmental phase.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud was observed from the NWS office out the west window for a couple of minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail produce some minor crop damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weak tornado touched down briefly, less than a minute.  In that time, the tornado managed to destroy a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of March 10, an approaching weak mid-level wave brought a brief pulse of warm advection. With cold temperatures at the surface and warmer temperatures aloft, a mix of precipitation types occurred during the morning and midday hours before changing over to light snow.  While most snowfall was less than an inch, a narrow band of one to four inches occurred from near De Smet to Chester and Flandreau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced ice accumulations up to a tenth of an inch in the morning. Precipitation ended as snowfall, with accumulations mainly less than an inch.

A large cluster of thunderstorms developed across central South Dakota during the overnight and moved into southeast South Dakota late at night. An isolated storm pulsed stronger along the exposed southern flank and impacted areas near Interstate 90 with gusty winds.

A near record amount of moisture in the atmosphere was available to fuel persistent storms which developed late afternoon September 19, and continued through much of the overnight and early morning of September 20.  A widespread 4 to 9 inches of rain occurred across southeast South Dakota, with some unofficial amounts up to 13 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several paved county roads were underwater around Davis, including 461st Avenue.   Several gravel roads also underwater, some impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall, ice breakup, and additional runoff from melting snow from March 23-24, pushed many areas along the Big Sioux River into minor flood stage at the end of the month. Waters mainly impacted agricultural lowlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rainfall, snow melt, and ice breakup caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along middle portions of the Big Sioux River. The river reached a crest of 0.9 feet above flood stage near Dell Rapids (gage site DERS2) on March 24.

An early Spring storm moved out of the Rockies, swinging across Nebraska early on the morning of the 24th. Precipitation blossomed within the leading warm advection region, including a few thunderstorms on the evening of the 23rd, which transitioned from rain to snow lifting northeast toward east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, starting as a brief period of rain, became heavy at times late evening on March 23 and into the early morning hours of March 24. Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, with snowfall accumulating to 6 to 9 inches, including 9.0 inches near Brookings and White, 7.8 inches 8 miles south of Brookings, 7.2 inches at Flandreau and 6.5 inches 10 miles north of Colman.

Locations with a foot or more include 12 inches at Presho, 13 inches at Miller, 14 inches at Gann Valley, 17 inches at Kennebec, 18 inches at Clear Lake, and 20 inches near Gary.  The highest measured wind gust was 66 mph at Danforth in southern Hand county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Spring storm moved out of the Rockies, swinging across Nebraska early on the morning of the 24th. Precipitation blossomed within the leading warm advection region, including a few thunderstorms near and south of Sioux Falls on the evening of the 23rd, which transitioned from rain to snow lifting northeast across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, starting as a brief period of rain, became moderate to briefly heavy by late evening on March 23 and into the early morning hours of March 24. Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, with snowfall accumulating to 2 to 5 inches, including 5.4 inches near Chester, 5.0 inches at Madison, 4.0 inches at Howard, 3.5 inches at De Smet, 2.7 inches at Sioux Falls and 2.0 inches at Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6.  500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened.  As a result, winds increased to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and produced whiteout conditions at times.  The system was dynamic enough to produce thunderstorms with heavy rainfall early on March 5 toward far eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 40 mph near Vermillion with snowfall of 5 to 7 inches, producing whiteout conditions. SDDOT closed Interstate 29 at 1830CST March 5 through early morning on March 6. Winter road maintenance was suspended in all areas due to whiteout conditions from late on March 5 through early on March 6. Most schools were closed or released early on March 5 and closed March 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall brought middle portions of the Big Sioux River above flood stage in early March. Crests were at minor flood stage near Hawarden (HWDI4) and Akron (AKRI4), impacting only lowland agricultural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along middle portions of the Big Sioux River. The river reached a crest of 1.7 feet above flood stage near Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) on March 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare mid-April blizzard affected most of central and northeast South Dakota from April 13th into April 15th. Heavy snowfall along with high winds brought widespread blizzard conditions to the region. Snowfall amounts from 6 to as much as 20 inches along with wind gusts to over 60 mph brought hazardous, if not impossible travel. In fact, both Interstate-29 and Interstate-90 were closed due to the heavy snow and blizzard conditions.  Parts of both Interstates were re-opened on the 14th, while the rest opened on the morning of the 15th.||Dozens of schools were closed on the 13th along with many state government offices. Many activities and events were also postponed or cancelled. Despite the cancellations and delays, the South Dakota Highway patrol still responded to hundreds of calls for service to assist stranded motorists.  The wet conditions/mud, snow, and wind greatly affected cattle producers trying to protect newborn calves. As a result of the blizzard there were some calf losses across the region. The winds subsided on the 14th for many locations, leading to improved visibility, while periods of snow continued into the 15th across eastern South Dakota. ||Snowfall amounts include 6 inches at Onida and Watertown, 7 inches at Murdo, Highmore, and Pierre, 8 inches at Clark and 6 miles east of Hayes, 9 inches at Milbank and Orient, 10 inches at Seneca and 12 miles north of Highmore, and 11 inches at Castlewood and near Vivian.  Locations with a foot or more include 12 inches at Presho, 13 inches at Miller, 14 inches at Gann Valley, 17 inches at Kennebec, 18 inches at Clear Lake, and 20 inches near Gary.  The highest measured wind gust was 66 mph at Danforth in southern Hand county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary very late in the afternoon near the Nebraska border.  These storms were slow moving with weak flow aloft and anchored on this nearly stationary boundary through the evening hours.  Precipitable water approached 2 inches with up to 3500 J/kg MLCAPE south of the boundary.  A band of rainfall from 3 to 5 inches occurred during the evening hours from near Gregory to Parkston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With over 2.50 inches rainfall by 1800CST, water was flowing well over the curbs in Burke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary very late in the afternoon near the Nebraska border. Precipitable water approached 2 inches with up to 3500 J/kg MLCAPE south of the boundary.  A band of rainfall from 3 to 5 inches occurred during the evening hours from near Gregory to Parkston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rain washed out a culvert on 413th Avenue around 100 yards south of 272nd Street during the overnight hours.  Around 0940CST the next morning, a driver ignored road closed barricades at the intersection and proceeded around 100 yards before crashing into the washout. The car ended up suspended by the bumpers across the gap in the roadway.  The 35-year old female driver was transported to the hospital with serious injuries, but a younger juvenile passenger secured in child restraints was uninjured in the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas from far southern South Dakota and northeast Nebraska into adjacent northwest Iowa were on the northern fringes of an area of low pressure moving across the Central Plains.  Light ice accumulations were followed by light snowfall, resulting in slick roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel conditions producing several minor accidents resulted from a period of light freezing rain which brought ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch. Light snowfall followed with 1 to 3 inches of accumulation from near Interstate 90 southward and west of the James River.
