The wet conditions/mud, snow, and wind greatly affected cattle producers trying to protect newborn calves. As a result of the blizzard there were some calf losses across the region. The winds subsided on the 14th for many locations, leading to improved visibility, while periods of snow continued into the 15th across eastern South Dakota. ||Snowfall amounts include 6 inches at Onida and Watertown, 7 inches at Murdo, Highmore, and Pierre, 8 inches at Clark and 6 miles east of Hayes, 9 inches at Milbank and Orient, 10 inches at Seneca and 12 miles north of Highmore, and 11 inches at Castlewood and near Vivian.  Locations with a foot or more include 12 inches at Presho, 13 inches at Miller, 14 inches at Gann Valley, 17 inches at Kennebec, 18 inches at Clear Lake, and 20 inches near Gary.  The highest measured wind gust was 66 mph at Danforth in southern Hand county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Spring storm moved out of the Rockies, swinging across Nebraska early on the morning of the 24th. Precipitation blossomed within the leading warm advection region, including a few thunderstorms near and south of Sioux Falls on the evening of the 23rd, which transitioned from rain to snow lifting northeast across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, starting as a brief period of rain, became moderate to briefly heavy by late evening on March 23 and into the early morning hours of March 24. Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, with snowfall accumulating to 2 to 5 inches, including 5.4 inches near Chester, 5.0 inches at Madison, 4.0 inches at Howard, 3.5 inches at De Smet, 2.7 inches at Sioux Falls and 2.0 inches at Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of March 10, an approaching weak mid-level wave brought a brief pulse of warm advection. With cold temperatures at the surface and warmer temperatures aloft, a mix of precipitation types occurred during the morning and midday hours before changing over to light snow.  While most snowfall was less than an inch, a narrow band of one to four inches occurred from near De Smet to Chester and Flandreau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch around Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of March 10, an approaching weak mid-level wave brought a brief pulse of warm advection. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain produced ice accumulation to around a tenth of an inch near De Smet before changing over to light snow.  Snowfall totaled a half inch at De Smet, and 3.0 inches 9 miles south southwest of Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6.  500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened.  As a result, winds increased to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and produced whiteout conditions at times.  The system was dynamic enough to produce thunderstorms with heavy rainfall early on March 5 toward far eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 43 mph at Sioux Falls with snowfall up to 3.5 inches at Canton and 4 inches on the south side of Sioux Falls, producing whiteout conditions. SDDOT closed Interstate 29 at 1830CST through early morning on March 6, and winter road maintenance was suspended in all areas due to whiteout conditions. Most schools were closed or released early on March 5 and closed March 6.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous winter weather evolved during the morning of March 5, as rain changed to light to moderate snowfall and was accompanied by winds gusting as high as 43 mph at Sioux Falls, 37 mph at Madison and 35 mph at Brookings. There were brief blizzard or near blizzard conditions during the midday hours around Sioux Falls, but not persistent enough to be defined as a blizzard. Total snowfall was generally 3 to 6 inches, including 4.2 inches at Brookings. Flandreau and Sioux Falls, and 3.6 inches 2 miles north of Chester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt along with periods of rainfall through mid March caused minor flooding along portions of the James River.  Impacts were found generally in lowland agricultural locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along portions of the James River. The river reached a crest of 1.1 feet above flood stage near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) on March 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level wave pushed southeast toward southwest Minnesota overnight and the early morning hours on July 19.  A large cluster of storms developed across eastern South Dakota after midnight, and with a high moisture content to the air mass, rainfall rates were quite high.  One storm on the southern edge of the complex intensified, with strong 0-3km shear with low-level jet contributing to a brief meso-vortex development as the storm moved into the southern side of Brookings, SD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixmile Creek at gaging site SMCS2 rapidly rose above flood stage at 0400CST and reached moderate flood stage at 0745CST.  Rainfall of 6.83 inches was measured 2 miles northeast of Brookings.  Numerous township roads were flooded north of Brookings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid drying of the topsoil due to hot temperatures in May along lack of rainfall resulted in severe drought conditions developing in the James Valley in the first week of June. In fact this area received a few dust storms when strong winds moved into the region. Major crop damage occurred mainly to soybeans in the severe drought area as a result of the dust storm and dryness. The James Valley had much above normal temperatures for the month of June averaging 6 to 7 degrees above normal. With the lack of rainfall throughout June, the severe drought expanded some south by the end of the month. Only small portions of Day, Clark, and Hand counties were in severe drought conditions in June.

A cold front moving in from the west brought severe weather to parts of central and north central South Dakota. Large hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds to 70 mph, along with flash flooding occurred during the morning hours. The big story was a long track destructive hailstorm and severe winds that started in southeast Dewey county and moved southeast through western Sully county and through Hughes County. The hail swath was nearly continuous and was from 2 to 5 miles wide and for nearly sixty miles. Tens of thousand of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm along with some wildlife killed including pheasants and deer. The hail and wind also wreaked havoc on buildings, windows, and vehicles. A second destructive hail and wind storm would cross this area on June 29th.

A second long track severe thunderstorm moved out of western South Dakota and across central South Dakota through the evening hours. This storm produced large hail up to 2 inches along with winds to nearly 90 mph as it moved across the region. A nearly continuous path of destruction occurred from northwest Stanley county east through central Sully county and across northern Hyde county. The path was nearly 90 miles across this area with a path width varying from 2 to 5 miles. Tens of thousands of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm with some farmers losing up to 15,000 acres alone.||The hail and wind storm also wreaked havoc on vehicles, buildings, and windows. Campers were toppled, grain bins and building siding were damaged with some roofs being blown off some structures.  Also, some wildlife, including birds and deer, were killed from the storm. Thirty-two power poles were downed west of Onida along with three transmission poles broken or leaning, leaving many without electricity through the night. Power lines and poles on the roads halted travel for some areas. This storm crossed some of the same areas in Sully county that were hit by destructive hail on June 27th. This hail and wind storm had began in Wyoming and traveled over 420 miles before it ended in eastern South Dakota.

Some fences and outside furniture were damaged along with several homes and businesses. There were signs blown over, windows blown out, along with several reports of vehicle damage. The creek then crested at 16.34 feet in the mid morning hours to just over major flood stage. Oak Creek then fell slowly into the afternoon and evening hours to below flood stage. Some agricultural land and roads were flooded.

The training of the thunderstorms over the same area brought extreme rainfall amounts of 4 to 11 inches across parts of northern Hand and southern Spink counties. Some rainfall amounts included, 6 inches 2 miles west of Tulare, 7.05 inches in Polo, 8.40 inches 2 miles southwest of Polo, 10.50 inches 2.5 miles southeast of Polo, and 11 inches 3 miles south southwest of Polo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme rainfall of eleven inches caused flash flooding with many roads under water.

A second long track severe thunderstorm moved out of western South Dakota and across central South Dakota through the evening hours. This storm produced large hail up to 2 inches along with winds to nearly 90 mph as it moved across the region. A nearly continuous path of destruction occurred from northwest Stanley county east through central Sully county and across northern Hyde county. The path was nearly 90 miles across this area with a path width varying from 2 to 5 miles. Tens of thousands of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm with some farmers losing up to 15,000 acres alone.||The hail and wind storm also wreaked havoc on vehicles, buildings, and windows. Campers were toppled, grain bins and building siding were damaged with some roofs being blown off some structures.  Also, some wildlife, including birds and deer, were killed from the storm. Thirty-two power poles were downed west of Onida along with three transmission poles broken or leaning, leaving many without electricity through the night. Power lines and poles on the roads halted travel for some areas. This storm crossed some of the same areas in Sully county that were hit by destructive hail on June 27th. This hail and wind storm had began in Wyoming and traveled over 420 miles before it ended in eastern South Dakota.

Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding across parts of northwestern Fall River County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain in two hours caused minor flooding. Runoff washed gravel off a couple of county roads and water ran over at least one county road when the culvert became plugged with debris. The Cheyenne River at Edgemont quickly rose about 5 feet and peaked around 1700 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain over Rapid City. The runoff caused flash flooding in parts of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms moved over the Rapid City area in the late afternoon and dropped two to three inches of rain in about an hour. Runoff from the heavy rain flooded streets and low-lying areas. Rapid Creek quickly overflowed its banks near the fairgrounds. The heaviest rain fell over the Robbinsdale area, where the runoff filled a detention pond near Robbinsdale Park to a depth of 30 feet and tore up sections of asphalt on Fairlane Drive. Several vehicles were pushed by the rushing water and one vehicle ended up in the detention pond. The fire department had to perform several water rescues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain over Rapid City. The runoff caused flash flooding in parts of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.68 inches fell in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level wave pushed across the northern Plains on May 8, with a frontal zone moving in during the evening hours. Even with modest instability with MLCAPE from 500-1000 J/kg, enough shear focused along the advancing boundary to allow a severe storm to produce large hail and a brief tornado.
A slow moving storm developed over parts of the northern and central Black Hills, producing large hail and heavy rain over the Rochford area. The runoff caused minor flooding around Rochford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to golf ball size covered the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork Rapid Creek was out of its banks approximately three to six miles west-northwest of Rochford. Several fields were covered with deep hail and water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the fringe of the stronger elevated mixed-layer, a few storms developed on a weak convergence boundary late day in proximity to strong surface-based instability.  Deep layer shear was weak, favoring organization into pulse multicell storms.  Isolated storms produced severe hail during the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground. Most hail less than quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the fringe of the stronger elevated mixed-layer, a few storms developed on a weak convergence boundary late day in proximity to strong surface-based instability.  Deep layer shear was weak, favoring organization into pulse multicell storms.  Isolated storms produced severe hail during the late afternoon hours.
Water from snow melt and rainfall continued to work through the Big Sioux River early in May.  Flooding remained generally minor with impacts limited to agricultural areas near the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow fall through minor flood stages persisted along much of the Big Sioux River during much of the first week of May, with impacts generally limited to flooding of agricultural areas near the river. Flooding ended at Akron (gage site AKRI4) on May 6.

A surface low pressure area moving across the region along with a cold front dropping in from the north brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. Damaging winds were the main occurrence throughout this event with only a few hail reports. Severe winds gusted up to around 100 mph at times as the storms moved quickly east through the evening and early morning hours. As a result, there were many reports of damage to structures and trees across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated eighty mph winds tipped a mobile home over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving in from the west brought severe weather to parts of central and north central South Dakota. Large hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds to 70 mph, along with flash flooding occurred during the morning hours. The big story was a long track destructive hailstorm and severe winds that started in southeast Dewey county and moved southeast through western Sully county and through Hughes County. The hail swath was nearly continuous and was from 2 to 5 miles wide and for nearly sixty miles. Tens of thousand of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm along with some wildlife killed including pheasants and deer. The hail and wind also wreaked havoc on buildings, windows, and vehicles. A second destructive hail and wind storm would cross this area on June 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail and damaging winds destroyed thousands of acres of crops and pastureland as a severe thunderstorm moved out of southeast Dewey county, across Sully county and through Hughes county. Some property damage also resulted from the severe winds and hail.

The outflow from the weakening line of storms resulted in high winds and a dust stormfor locations along and east of the James Valley.  See the dust storm entry for June 1st.

A funnel cloud was spotted in Hand county about 12 miles south of Ree Heights.

Severe thunderstorms developed in advance of a surface low pressure area and cold front in far northeast South Dakota. Very heavy rains of up to 6 inches, nickel hail, along with a brief tornado touchdown occurred during the late afternoon and early evening. The main areas of flash flooding were near Claire City in northern Roberts county and near Albee and Revillo in Grant county.
Rainfall averaging from 4 to 7 inches around the Sioux Falls metro area resulted in ponding of water in small creek basins and some poor drainage urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over Madison Street at a depth of 8 to 10 inches east of La Mesa Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of Ellis has standing water, and water flowing over gravel roads west of Ellis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a stationary front and brought large hail, damaging winds, flooding, along with a few tornadoes to parts of central and northeast South Dakota. Two tornadoes and large hail caused the most damage near Burdette in Hand county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado touched down picking up a substantial calving barn and rolling and tossing it about 100 feet to the northeast. The calving barn destroyed a fence line and severely damaged a chicken coop and large tree in the process. Sheet metal was removed from the top side of a separate barn. Additional debris was tossed to the southeast about 150 to 200 feet, bending a fence. Chemical and water storage tanks were tossed about 50 feet to the north. Hay bales were removed from stacks and tossed/rolled to the east with a bale in a feeder picked up and destroyed.  Large hail dented the siding and broke windows on both houses on the farmstead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area moving across the region along with a cold front dropping in from the north brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. Damaging winds were the main occurrence throughout this event with only a few hail reports. Severe winds gusted up to around 100 mph at times as the storms moved quickly east through the evening and early morning hours. As a result, there were many reports of damage to structures and trees across the region.

A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days.  With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 3 to as much as 10 inches.  Sioux Falls, South Dakota established a new 24-hour rainfall record of 5.07 ending 1600LST September 20, as well as daily records for both September 19 and 20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop and pasture land was inundated, as well as portions of the Flandreau Golf Course, as levels on Flandreau Creek above Flandreau climbed to 1.1 feet above flood stage (gage site FCRS2) on September 21. The river returned quickly below flood stage on September 22.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on September 18-20 brought a widespread 3 to 5 inches of rainfall with 4.66 inches at Pipestone. EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the second time in the month, heavy rainfall occurred across the Brule Creek basin from September 19-21, bringing 3 to 5 inches accumulation and moderate flooding.  478th Avenue and several other township roads across Emmett township were inundated  by water as Brule Creek near Richland (gage site BCKS2) crested 3.9 feet above flood stage around 0900LST on September 22.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: With areas north of Sioux Falls just on the northern edge of heaviest rainfall from September 18-20, amounts generally 4 to 7 inches resulted in minor flooding of crop and pasture land around 60th Street North and Interstate 90. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving in from the west brought severe weather to parts of central and north central South Dakota. Large hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds to 70 mph, along with flash flooding occurred during the morning hours. The big story was a long track destructive hailstorm and severe winds that started in southeast Dewey county and moved southeast through western Sully county and through Hughes County. The hail swath was nearly continuous and was from 2 to 5 miles wide and for nearly sixty miles. Tens of thousand of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm along with some wildlife killed including pheasants and deer. The hail and wind also wreaked havoc on buildings, windows, and vehicles. A second destructive hail and wind storm would cross this area on June 29th.

A line of severe storms storms developed along a cold front in central South Dakota sweeping east across the region during the afternoon and evening. Large hail over 2 inches along with winds gusting to 80 mph occurred with these storms.  A brief weak tornado also touched down just northeast of Glenham. The outflow from the weakening line of storms resulted in high winds and a dust stormfor locations along and east of the James Valley.  See the dust storm entry for June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched downed briefly just northeast of Glenham. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving in from the west brought severe weather to parts of central and north central South Dakota. Large hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds to 70 mph, along with flash flooding occurred during the morning hours. The big story was a long track destructive hailstorm and severe winds that started in southeast Dewey county and moved southeast through western Sully county and through Hughes County. A second long track severe thunderstorm moved out of western South Dakota and across central South Dakota through the evening hours. This storm produced large hail up to 2 inches along with winds to nearly 90 mph as it moved across the region. A nearly continuous path of destruction occurred from northwest Stanley county east through central Sully county and across northern Hyde county. The path was nearly 90 miles across this area with a path width varying from 2 to 5 miles. Tens of thousands of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm with some farmers losing up to 15,000 acres alone.||The hail and wind storm also wreaked havoc on vehicles, buildings, and windows. Campers were toppled, grain bins and building siding were damaged with some roofs being blown off some structures.  Also, some wildlife, including birds and deer, were killed from the storm. Thirty-two power poles were downed west of Onida along with three transmission poles broken or leaning, leaving many without electricity through the night. Power lines and poles on the roads halted travel for some areas. This storm crossed some of the same areas in Sully county that were hit by destructive hail on June 27th. This hail and wind storm had began in Wyoming and traveled over 420 miles before it ended in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seventy mph winds were estimated.

A few farm buildings were flooded with widespread crop and pasture land flooding as the Big Sioux River near Richland (gage site BSRS2) crested 6.2 feet above flood stage on September 23.  The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days.  With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 3 to as much as 10 inches.  Sioux Falls, South Dakota established a new 24-hour rainfall record of 5.07 ending 1600LST September 20, as well as daily records for both September 19 and 20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread upstream rainfall in the Big Sioux basin from September 18-20 totaled generally 5 to 10 inches, with downstream routing resulting in minor flooding. Significant flooding of crop and pasture land occured as the Big Sioux River near Jefferson (gage site BSJS2) crested 4.8 feet above flood stage on September 25.

Sioux Falls, South Dakota established a new 24-hour rainfall record of 5.07 ending 1600LST September 20, as well as daily records for both September 19 and 20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall around the Sioux Falls metro area from September 18-20 totaled generally 5 to 7 inches. Minor flooding impacted mainly rural agricultural land, but water did start flowing over West 12th Street west of Sertoma Avenue along with some lowland flooding of city parks. Skunk Creek at Sioux Falls (gage site SIFS2) crested 1.0 foot above flood stage on September 21.

A surface low pressure area moving across the region along with a cold front dropping in from the north brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. Damaging winds were the main occurrence throughout this event with only a few hail reports. Severe winds gusted up to around 100 mph at times as the storms moved quickly east through the evening and early morning hours. As a result, there were many reports of damage to structures and trees across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe winds of over ninety mph damaged several buildings and grain bins along with downing multiple power poles.

Precipitable water was near record levels across the immediate region for this time, with a strong low-level jet interacting with a nearly stationary west to east boundary as several weak disturbances progressed northeast across the area. Three to seven inches of rain fell through the Vermillion River basin on September 18-20. A fairly rapid rise into minor to moderate flooding occurred as a result of several periods of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic heavy rainfall from September 18 to September 20 resulted in a widespread accumulation from 3 to 7 inches. Moderate flooding occurred, with U.S. Highway 18 west of Davis inundated for a few days along with significant amounts of crop and pasture land, as the Vermillion River near Davis (gage site DVSS2) crested 3.1 feet above flood stage on September 22. The river dropped below flood stage on September 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases continued from upstream dams along the Missouri River and kept much of the stretch between Fort Randall Dam and Gavins Point Dam in minor flood stage at times during October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of mainly agricultural areas occurred as the Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1) returned above flood stage mid month. A few recreational homes between Verdel and Niobrara were impacted by high water levels. The river crested 0.3 feet above flood stage on October 13, then quickly dropped below flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the northern Black Hills and traveled southeast along the eastern slopes of the Black Hills and onto the adjacent plains before weakening. The storm produced large hail and wind gusts to 60 mph.

A cold front moving in from the west brought severe weather to parts of central and north central South Dakota. Large hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds to 70 mph, along with flash flooding occurred during the morning hours. The big story was a long track destructive hailstorm and severe winds that started in southeast Dewey county and moved southeast through western Sully county and through Hughes County. The hail swath was nearly continuous and was from 2 to 5 miles wide and for nearly sixty miles. Tens of thousand of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm along with some wildlife killed including pheasants and deer. The hail and wind also wreaked havoc on buildings, windows, and vehicles. A second destructive hail and wind storm would cross this area on June 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were estimated.

A line of severe storms storms developed along a cold front in central South Dakota sweeping east across the region during the afternoon and evening. Large hail over 2 inches along with winds gusting to 80 mph occurred with these storms.  A brief weak tornado also touched down just northeast of Glenham. The outflow from the weakening line of storms resulted in high winds and a dust stormfor locations along and east of the James Valley.  See the dust storm entry for June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated eighty mph wind from a thunderstorm brought down a tree. Before the rain began, blowing dust occurred with the visibility down to an eighth of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong outflow winds from a weakening line of thunderstorms along with the severe drought conditions brought a dust storm to areas in and just west of the James River Valley. Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph stirred up large amounts of dust bringing the visibility down to an eighth of mile or less at times during the evening hours. Hundreds of acres of soybeans were damaged by the winds and dust and had to be replanted. The high winds caused some downed power lines at Richmond Lake which caused some trees to start on fire. There were also several traffic accidents along with some vehicles ending up in the ditch due to the poor visibility. Some of the more notable accidents all occurred on Highway 14. There was a rear end collision that occurred with no injuries. The second accident was a rear end trailer that tipped over due to the high winds. The third accident was a head on collision in Hyde county with some injuries occurring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases continued from upstream dams along the Missouri River and kept the stretch between Fort Randall Dam and Gavins Point Dam in minor flood stage for most of the month.  For a brief period late month, scheduled releases were diminished to allow for downstream flow to clear the lower reaches of the river and allowed for the river to return briefly to within banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some boat docks near Niobrara were flooded along with minor impacts to mainly agricultural areas during most of the month as the Missouri River at Niobrara (gage site MRNN1) crested 1.2 feet above flood stage on September 20. Other than a brief period from September 22 to September 24, the river remained in minor flood through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Other than a brief period from September 20 to September 27, minor flooding of mainly agricultural areas persisted through the entire month along the Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2). The river crested at 0.5 feet above flood stage on September 19. The river dropped below flood stage at 0800CST September 30.

A second long track severe thunderstorm moved out of western South Dakota and across central South Dakota through the evening hours. This storm produced large hail up to 2 inches along with winds to nearly 90 mph as it moved across the region. A nearly continuous path of destruction occurred from northwest Stanley county east through central Sully county and across northern Hyde county. The path was nearly 90 miles across this area with a path width varying from 2 to 5 miles. Tens of thousands of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm with some farmers losing up to 15,000 acres alone.||The hail and wind storm also wreaked havoc on vehicles, buildings, and windows. Campers were toppled, grain bins and building siding were damaged with some roofs being blown off some structures.  Also, some wildlife, including birds and deer, were killed from the storm. Thirty-two power poles were downed west of Onida along with three transmission poles broken or leaning, leaving many without electricity through the night. Power lines and poles on the roads halted travel for some areas. This storm crossed some of the same areas in Sully county that were hit by destructive hail on June 27th. This hail and wind storm had began in Wyoming and traveled over 420 miles before it ended in eastern South Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent rains through September 4, lacking significant rainfall rates in a largely moist neutral thermodynamic profile, produced locally heavier amounts from 2 to 4 inches from far southeast South Dakota into adjacent sections of northwest Iowa.  Smaller creeks and streams showed stronger response to the rainfall by the end of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A day of periodic moderate rain bringing around 2 inches of rainfall to Vermillion, 2.89 inches to Akron and 3.31 inches near Richland resulted in major flooding by late evening on September 4, inundating 478th Avenue and several other township roads across Emmett township. Brule Creek near Richland (gage site BCKS2) crested 6.0 feet above flood stage around 0200CST on September 5, falling very quickly below flood stage by 1000CST.

A second long track severe thunderstorm moved out of western South Dakota and across central South Dakota through the evening hours. This storm produced large hail up to 2 inches along with winds to nearly 90 mph as it moved across the region. A nearly continuous path of destruction occurred from northwest Stanley county east through central Sully county and across northern Hyde county. The path was nearly 90 miles across this area with a path width varying from 2 to 5 miles. Tens of thousands of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm with some farmers losing up to 15,000 acres alone.||The hail and wind storm also wreaked havoc on vehicles, buildings, and windows. Campers were toppled, grain bins and building siding were damaged with some roofs being blown off some structures.  Also, some wildlife, including birds and deer, were killed from the storm. Thirty-two power poles were downed west of Onida along with three transmission poles broken or leaning, leaving many without electricity through the night. Power lines and poles on the roads halted travel for some areas. This storm crossed some of the same areas in Sully county that were hit by destructive hail on June 27th. This hail and wind storm had began in Wyoming and traveled over 420 miles before it ended in eastern South Dakota.

Three to five inches of rain fell over the Badlands and southwest South Dakota from June 17th through the 20th. Runoff caused minor flooding along the White River from Kadoka to the confluence of the Missouri River. The river was only above flood stage at Oacoma for a short time period on June 21st when it rose to just above the flood stage of 15 feet to 15.6 feet. Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two apartments extensively damaged by flood waters on lower level.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong outflow winds from a weakening line of thunderstorms along with the severe drought conditions brought a dust storm to areas in and just west of the James River Valley. Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph stirred up large amounts of dust bringing the visibility down to an eighth of mile or less at times during the evening hours. Hundreds of acres of soybeans were damaged by the winds and dust and had to be replanted. The high winds caused some downed power lines at Richmond Lake which caused some trees to start on fire. There were also several traffic accidents along with some vehicles ending up in the ditch due to the poor visibility. Some of the more notable accidents all occurred on Highway 14. There was a rear end collision that occurred with no injuries. The second accident was a rear end trailer that tipped over due to the high winds. The third accident was a head on collision in Hyde county with some injuries occurring.

A surface low pressure area moving across the region along with a cold front dropping in from the north brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. Damaging winds were the main occurrence throughout this event with only a few hail reports. Severe winds gusted up to around 100 mph at times as the storms moved quickly east through the evening and early morning hours. As a result, there were many reports of damage to structures and trees across the region.

A line of severe storms storms developed along a cold front in central South Dakota sweeping east across the region during the afternoon and evening. Large hail over 2 inches along with winds gusting to 80 mph occurred with these storms.  A brief weak tornado also touched down just northeast of Glenham. The outflow from the weakening line of storms resulted in high winds and a dust stormfor locations along and east of the James Valley.  See the dust storm entry for June 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases continued from upstream dams along the Missouri River and kept much of the stretch between Fort Randall Dam and Gavins Point Dam in minor flood stage at times during October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increased releases from Fort Randall Dam resulted in a return to minor flooding of mainly agricultural areas at the start of October. The Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2) crested at 0.5 feet above flood stage on October 13. The river dropped below flood stage on October 14, then returned briefly above flood stage from October 21 to October 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases continued from upstream dams along the Missouri River and kept much of the stretch between Fort Randall Dam and Gavins Point Dam in minor flood stage at times during October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of mainly agricultural areas occurred as the Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1) returned above flood stage mid month. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated seventy mph winds brought many tree branches down along with knocking out power for about half of Faulkton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across the region bringing severe thunderstorms with damaging winds, large hail, and flooding to parts of central and northeast South Dakota.

A cold front moving in from the west brought severe weather to parts of central and north central South Dakota. Large hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds to 70 mph, along with flash flooding occurred during the morning hours. The big story was a long track destructive hailstorm and severe winds that started in southeast Dewey county and moved southeast through western Sully county and through Hughes County. The hail swath was nearly continuous and was from 2 to 5 miles wide and for nearly sixty miles. Tens of thousand of acres of crops and pastureland were destroyed by this storm along with some wildlife killed including pheasants and deer. The hail and wind also wreaked havoc on buildings, windows, and vehicles. A second destructive hail and wind storm would cross this area on June 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail and damaging winds destroyed many acres of crops and pastureland as a severe thunderstorm moved southeast out of Dewey into northwest Sully county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased releases from upstream reservoirs to route heavy spring runoff downstream contributed to minor flooding between Fort Randall and Gavins Point Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of mainly agricultural areas occurred as the Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1) remained above flood stage for most of the month.  The river crested 0.4 feet above flood stage on August 16, but dropped briefly below flood stage from August 20 to August 23. The river remained in minor flood through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave and jet entrance region pushed into the Northern Plains, with a tight mid-level frontal boundary stalling across the area for several hours within confluent southwest flow aloft.  Unseasonably cold air rushed into the region behind a southward surging low-level frontal boundary,  with developing precipitation making a very rapid transition from rain to snow.  Snow accumulated quickly in the overnight and early morning hours due to proximity of slightly unstable air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snow accumulated to 2 to 6 inches across portions of southeast South Dakota near and south of Interstate 90. Snowfall of 3.5 inches was measured 6 miles northwest of Montrose, with 3.1 inches at Montrose and 2.0 inches at Bridgewater and 5 miles northeast of Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough sliding southeast across the Northern Plains generated numerous thunderstorms during the later afternoon and evening hours across central South Dakota. While the environment was less favorable with a much weaker shear profile toward southeast South Dakota, an isolated storm pulsed in strength near Chamberlain and produced wind damage before rapidly weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts at the leading edge of the storm blew a plastic play set across the yard, ripped numerous shingles off the roof and broke drain spouts. Ground was also covered by pea size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front trudged southward across the area during the late morning and early afternoon, a few slow-moving thunderstorms developed and became anchored for a brief time across southwestern portions of the Sioux Falls metro area.  Rainfall rates in excess of 4 inches per hour occurred, with some significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of stalled cars and flash flooding at both the intersection of 41st Street and Marion Road and the intersection of 41st Street and Larch Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm developed during the evening across central South Dakota and progressed into the James River valley north of Huron late in the evening.  With an appreciable amount of DCAPE, the storm produced damaging wind before weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches up to two inches in diameter were broken from trees.

A severe thunderstorm developed rapidly on the west side of Rapid City and moved eastward through the downtown area and into Rapid Valley. A cluster of severe storms then formed east of Rapid City and moved east-southeast across west central South Dakota. The storm produced golf ball sized hail in Rapid City, with a gust of 68 mph at the National Weather Service office, which caused extensive vehicle damage. Large hail and strong wind gusts continued east across the plains.

Thunderstorms formed along the fringes of an area of upper-level low pressure drifting across southern Nebraska during the afternoon.  Nearly saturated lapse rates through a deep layer promoted heavy rainfall from storms, which drifted northwest across the Sioux Falls metro area late in the afternoon.  Two cells merged across southwest Sioux Falls and produced excessive rainfall along with widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reported over curb height, with up to a foot of water over the road.

A weak disturbance moving northeast from Nebraska along with a weak surface trough ignited convection during the evening hours. As a weakening area of showers and storms moved into the Sioux Falls area, an isolated thunderstorm intensified and produced a small-scale burst of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down near the Sioux Empire Fairgrounds with damage to a few midway rides. Additional tree damage was reported near Elmwood Golf Course.  The Sioux Empire Fair was closed at 1035 pm prior to approach of the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave and jet entrance region pushed into the Northern Plains, with a tight mid-level frontal boundary stalling across the area for several hours within confluent southwest flow aloft.  Unseasonably cold air rushed into the region behind a southward surging low-level frontal boundary,  with developing precipitation making a very rapid transition from rain to snow.  Snow accumulated quickly in the overnight and early morning hours due to proximity of slightly unstable air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snow accumulated to 2 to 6 inches across portions of southeast South Dakota near and south of Interstate 90. Snowfall of 3.0 inches was measured near Lake Andes, with 2.0 inches at Platte.

After some improvement in the drought conditions in early July in the James Valley, much below normal rainfall in early August resulted in severe drought conditions redeveloping  by mid August.  By mid August, Aberdeen had only received six hundredths of an inch of rainfall for the month. The severe drought affected crops trying to recover from the earlier summer severe drought. The severe drought conditions continued into September.

Storms fired near a nearly stationary boundary across central to northeast South Dakota during the evening, brushing across areas northwest of Huron.  An effective CAPE around 1500 J/kg and 0-3km shear of 35-40 knots showed a preference to supercell structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While the main core of the storm remained north of the area, outflow winds swept through  the far northern portions of the county with some minor tree damage around Hitchcock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed along a weak convergence boundary during the later afternoon and early evening near the James River valley.  Thermodynamic profiles exhibited a narrow and limited CAPE, but decent acceleration potential for updrafts through the lowest couple of kilometers.

A few storms developed along a weak convergence boundary during the later afternoon and early evening near the James River valley.  Thermodynamic profiles exhibited a narrow and limited CAPE, but decent acceleration potential for updrafts through the lowest couple of kilometers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel was observed to briefly touch down in an open field, resulting in minimal damage to crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a frontal zone in south central SD late afternoon, despite a strong +14 C cap around 750 hPa.  The leading edge of larger-scale lift began to move in late afternoon with jet streak punching toward central Nebraska ahead of a deep western U.S. trough, allowing the environment to tap the nearly 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and utilize 30 to 35 knots of 0-3 km shear near the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large DCAPE values supported development of a strong cold pool with a thunderstorm, producing damaging winds measured at the K9D1 AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11.  Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A steady moderate rainfall during October 9-10 - resulting in 1.50 to 2.00 inches of rainfall - produced yet another period of moderate flooding. Significant amounts of agricultural land near the river were inundated, as well as several rural gravel roads near the river. The Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) crested 3.4 feet above flood stage on October 13.

Very large hail to baseball size fell from Lead and Deadwood to Tilford and Piedmont, causing significant damage.  The storm continued to be severe as it moved farther east across west central South Dakota, producing wind gusts to 70 mph.

An EF-1 tornado began in Wyoming and moved across west central Lawrence County into Spearfish Canyon before dissipating. Very large hail to baseball size fell from Lead and Deadwood to Tilford and Piedmont, causing significant damage.  The storm continued to be severe as it moved farther east across west central South Dakota, producing wind gusts to 70 mph.

Primed by 1 to 3 inches of rainfall on June 17-18, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 3 to 7 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20, 24 and 25. Specific weekly totals included 4.02 inches at Pipestone, 2.14 inches near Edgerton, and several reports around 8 inches near Garretson. Major flooding occurred with significant amounts of crop and pasture land inundated, along with several low water crossings as Split Rock Creek crested below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) 5.7 feet above flood stage on June 21. The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primed by 1 to 3 inches of rainfall on June 17-18, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20, 24 and 25. Specific weekly totals included 4.02 inches at Pipestone, 2.14 inches near Edgerton, 7.48 inches at EROS Data Center and several reports around 8 inches near Garretson. Moderate flooding occurred with significant amounts of crop and pasture land inundated along with a garden center near the confluence with the Big Sioux River as Split Rock Creek at Corson (gage site COSS2) crested 4.3 feet above flood stage on June 21. The river dropped below flood stage on June 22, but returned above flood stage and crested again at moderate flood stage 3.9 feet above flood stage on June 26. The river fell below flood stage on June 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11.  Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A steady moderate rainfall during October 9-10 - resulting in 1.50 to 2.00 inches of rainfall - produced yet another period of moderate flooding. South Dakota Highway 46 was again inundated, and several rural homes were isolated by high water. The Big Sioux River above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) crested 4.6 feet above flood stage on October 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With elevated instability up to 2000 J/kg, an isolated thunderstorm developed in southeast South Dakota north of the synoptic scale warm front across Nebraska which produced hail up to penny size.

A strong mid-level wave moved out across western South Dakota during the evening hours, initiating convection along a weak convergence boundary in an environment with up to 2500 J/kg MUCAPE and deep-layer shear of 35 kts. Storms progressed eastward and quickly formed a linear MCS which produced spotty damaging wind and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds on the leading edge of thunderstorms broke 3 inch diameter limbs from trees and snapped power poles.

Heavy rain from thunderstorms over several days caused flooding over portions of Fall River County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred across Fall River County. Flooded roads included Edgemont Road northwest of Ardmore, Hard Scrabble Road, Black Banks Road, and Horsehead Road. Numerous small creeks and streams were out of their banks; including Beef Creek, Beaver Creek, Black Banks Creek, Cottonwood Creek, Hat Creek, Plum Creek, Duck Creek, Alkali Creek, Little Cottonwood Creek, and tributaries into Hat Creek, Horsehead Creek, and the Cheyenne River upstream of Angostura Reservoir. Inflows into Angostura Reservoir went from around 250 cubic feet per second (cfs) to over 4500 cfs in just a couple of days. The high inflows raised the water elevation in the reservoir, so releases were increased to around 4000 cfs for a few days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water from snow melt and rainfall continued to work through the Big Sioux River early in May.  Flooding remained generally minor with impacts limited to agricultural areas near the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow fall through minor flood stages persisted along much of the Big Sioux River during much of the first week of May, with impacts generally limited to flooding of agricultural areas near the river. Flooding ended above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) on May 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent overrunning pattern produced periods of moderate to heavy rain from May 8 to May 11, bringing a widespread 2 to 4 inches rainfall to the middle portions of the Big Sioux River.  On top of already high flows, minor flooding resulted at many sites which just recently had pulled out of minor flood conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from periods of heavier rainfall on top of already high river levels produced minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along lower portions of the Big Sioux River. River levels reached 2.8 feet above flood stage above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) on May 13.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from periods of heavier rainfall on top of already high river levels produced minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along lower portions of the Big Sioux River. River levels reached 1.4 feet above flood stage at Akron (gage site AKRI4) on May 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme rainfall and severe weather occurred along a stationary frontal boundary extending across the region. Thunderstorms training over the same area brought rainfall amounts from 3 to over 13 inches causing extensive flash flooding. Many roads and cropland were flooded and damaged. Several roads had to be barricaded. There was some sandbagging at some farmsteads with several homes receiving water in their basements. The extreme rainfall also caused the dam at Lake Hiddenwood to burst sending water downstream resulting in more flash flooding. The entire lake was drained and water from the lake flowed for miles toward Sand Lake near the Walworth/Campbell county borders. The South Dakota governor requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration for Campbell, Walworth, and McPherson counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to the very heavy rainfall earlier, the dam at Lake Hiddenwood broke in the early morning hours spreading water northwest through a creek. Several roads were flooded along with crop and pastureland. The earthen dam was built in 1927 and was 27 feet high with a concrete spillway. The bridge over the spillway was also destroyed. The lake was one of the first artificial lakes in South Dakota. Lake Hiddenwood State Park was closed indefinitely.

A severe thunderstorm developed between Belle Fourche and Spearfish, then tracked southeast through portions of the northern Black Hills. The storm produced large hail and strong wind gusts along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed between Belle Fourche and Spearfish, then tracked southeast through portions of the northern Black Hills. The storm produced large hail and strong wind gusts along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the central Black Hills and tracked eastward through the Rapid City area before weakening over the plains. The storm initially contained large hail over the Black Hills, then also produced strong winds as it moved through the Rapid City area. Flash flooding also occurred in the Keystone and Rapid City areas due to two to three inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain continued to cause flooding in and around Keystone. Water covered Grizzly Gulch Road, parts of Cemetery Road, and parking lots of businesses along Iron Mountain Road. Levels on Grizzly Bear Creek and Battle Creek quickly rose several feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the central Black Hills and tracked eastward through the Rapid City area before weakening over the plains. The storm initially contained large hail over the Black Hills, then also produced strong winds as it moved through the Rapid City area. Flash flooding also occurred in the Keystone and Rapid City areas due to two to three inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred throughout Rapid City. Water was reported over Third Street, St. Francis Street, Meade Street, and West Main Street. Rapid Creek quickly rose and inundated lower portions of the bike path through Rapid City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the central Black Hills and tracked eastward through the Rapid City area before weakening over the plains. The storm initially contained large hail over the Black Hills, then also produced strong winds as it moved through the Rapid City area. Flash flooding also occurred in the Keystone and Rapid City areas due to two to three inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued along Rapid Creek, especially in Rapid Valley near Green Valley Road and Reservoir Road. The river gauge near the Water Reclamation facility crested slightly above flood stage with a peak flow around 1430 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over eastern Haakon County and tracked southeast across south central South Dakota. The storm produced large hail and strong wind gusts across parts of the area.

Large hail and strong winds also caused property damage just west of Spearfish.

Two semis collided in dense fog three miles west of Summit on U.S. Highway 12. One semi was driving east on Highway 12 when another semi was pulling off a side road when they collided.  Almost 16 tons of beef roasts were destroyed as a result of the collision. One of the drivers suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed near a surface low pressure area and dryline in central South Dakota during the late afternoon and evening hours. Very large hail up to three inches in diameter, damaging winds to seventy mph, along with a couple tornadoes occurred in central and north central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based off spotter photos and high resolution satellite imagery, the tornado tracked for over a half mile through a center pivot farm field around 19 miles north of Highmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal heating allowed a few thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon of May 25 within a zone of steep mid-level lapse rates.  With lack of deep-layer shear, storms were relatively short-lived, but one storm managed to produce hail up to quarter size.
A slow moving storm developed over parts of the northern and central Black Hills, producing large hail and heavy rain over the Rochford area. The runoff caused minor flooding around Rochford.
Strong winds accompanied a severe thunderstorm over portions of western Meade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer saw a large tree fall on Alkali Road due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of Alberta clipper systems moving across the plains left behind a swath of light to moderate snow accumulations across southeast South Dakota. Much of the area received from 3 to 6 inches snowfall, while wind chills dropped below zero by the end of the time of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, producing hazardous travel conditions with snowfall totaling as much as 5.2 inches near Pukwana, 5.0 inches near Wagner and 10 miles west of Dimock, 4.5 inches 9 miles north of Gregory, 4.0 inches at White Lake, 3.0 inches at Yankton and 2.8 inches at Tyndall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the higher elevations of the central Black Hills and tracked east-southeast through the foothills before dissipating over the plains. The storm produced large hail from near Sheridan Lake to Fairburn. Heavy rain also caused flash flooding in areas from Keystone to near Hermosa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain, exacerbated by hail covered roads, caused flash flooding in and around Keystone. Runoff also caused flooding around Mount Rushmore, where the walking trail was briefly inundated from water rushing down the stairs between the Grandview Terrace and the Sculpture Studio. Water covered Grizzly Gulch Road, parts of Cemetery Road, and several parking lots of businesses along Iron Mountain Road. River levels on Grizzly Bear Creek and Battle Creek quickly rose several feet.

The storm produced large hail from near Sheridan Lake to Fairburn. Heavy rain also caused flash flooding in areas from Keystone to near Hermosa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was observed along Battle Creek and Grace Coolidge Creek upstream of Highway 79. Several pastures and agricultural land were inundated. The river gauge on Battle Creek at Hermosa reached flood stage briefly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe over portions of Oglala Lakota County. The storm produced large hail at Sharps Corner.

Severe thunderstorms developed near a surface low pressure area and dryline in central South Dakota during the late afternoon and evening hours. Very large hail up to three inches in diameter, damaging winds to seventy mph, along with a couple tornadoes occurred in central and north central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado started on the west side of a ranch and threw a 4-wheeler into a barn. There was additional damage to trees and the roof of a barn at the ranch. The tornado moved across an open field and damaged a calf shed. It then crossed 180th street and 331st avenue and moved through a corn field. Many corn stalks were flattened in a 50 yard wide path before entering an open field and then crossing a hay field destroying a hay bale before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure trough from the southwest United States brought a couple rounds of snow to the region. The snow began in the morning hours of the 26th from midnight to noon and ended in the morning hours of the 28th. There was also mixed precipitation including freezing drizzle with the first wave.  Heavy snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 13 inches. Northwest winds increased to 25 to 40 mph in the morning and afternoon with gusts to over 50 mph on the 27th resulting in widespread blizzard conditions across much of the region, ending in the morning hours of the 28th.||Travel was greatly affected or completely halted with no travel advised across much of the region. Many reports of vehicles becoming stuck or ending up in the ditch occurred. There were also many activities and events postponed or cancelled along with many businesses closed. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Mobridge and Timber Lake; 7 inches at Roscoe, Pollock, Clear Lake, Gettysburg, and Columbia; 8 inches at Webster, Milbank, and Aberdeen; 9 inches at Clark; 10 inches at Watertown and Wilmot; 11 inches at Britton and Leola; 13 inches at Sisseton, Castlewood, and Milbank.

A warm front followed by an Alberta Clipper and Arctic front brought 1 to 6 inches of snow along with some freezing rain to northeast South Dakota from December 30th into the 31st. The freezing rain on the 30th was mainly from Redfield over to Watertown where a tenth to two tenths of an inch of ice occurred. This caused very slippery conditions. The strong north winds of 30 to 45 mph came in on the 31st in the morning bringing blizzard conditions to northeast South Dakota into the afternoon hours. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted by the ice and low visibility.

A large storm system brought snow and mixed precipitation to the area. The first part of the system brought several inches of snow from the Black Hills area to northwestern South Dakota. The second and stronger part of the system moved northeast across the Central Plains and brought snow from southwestern to south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow was primarily across far south central South Dakota, where around six inches of snow fell. Strong northwest winds produced blowing and drifting snow across much of the western South Dakota plains, especially south central South Dakota, with the second part of the system.

A strong cold front and upper level system crossed the region, bringing snow and gusty winds to a good portion of western South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills and adjacent plains, where amounts of eight inches to around a foot were reported. Elsewhere, four to eight inches of snow was common across much of southwest into west central South Dakota. Strong wind gusts to 50 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.

A winter storm tracked across the Central Plains, bringing accumulating snowfall to much of western and south central South Dakota. Most of southwestern and south central South Dakota, including the Black Hills, received five to nine inches of snow, with some areas over south central South Dakota receiving as much as 15 inches of snow. Winds were relatively light with this storm, but breezy conditions over south central South Dakota did produce some minor blowing and drifting snow. Accumulations were less across northwestern South Dakota, with parts of far northwestern South Dakota seeing very little snowfall.

Accumulations were less across northwestern South Dakota, with parts of far northwestern South Dakota seeing very little snowfall.

A warm front followed by an Alberta Clipper and Arctic front brought 1 to 6 inches of snow along with some freezing rain to northeast South Dakota from December 30th into the 31st. The freezing rain on the 30th was mainly from Redfield over to Watertown where a tenth to two tenths of an inch of ice occurred. This caused very slippery conditions. The strong north winds of 30 to 45 mph came in on the 31st in the morning bringing blizzard conditions to northeast South Dakota into the afternoon hours. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted by the ice and low visibility.

A large storm system brought snow and mixed precipitation to the area. The first part of the system brought several inches of snow from the Black Hills area to northwestern South Dakota. The second and stronger part of the system moved northeast across the Central Plains and brought snow from southwestern to south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow was primarily across far south central South Dakota, where around six inches of snow fell. Strong northwest winds produced blowing and drifting snow across much of the western South Dakota plains, especially south central South Dakota, with the second part of the system.

A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2.  The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning.  Heavy snow and wind developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to Interstate 29 in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain which produced a glaze of light icing during the early morning of December 1, heavy snow occurred for numerous hours.  With temperatures near freezing, snowfall was greatly elevation dependent, with amounts over less than 24 hours from 17.1 inches 2 miles northeast of Burke and 13.5 inches at Gregory, to 7.4 inches 4 miles south southwest of Pickstown.  Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph from 1800CST December 1 to 0300CST December 2, producing periods of visibility of a half mile or less and treacherous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a shortwave disturbance rotated around a large southwest U.S. closed low aloft and into confluent flow aloft across the Northern Plains, light wintry precipitation developed on December 31.  An arctic front surged southward early in the day with winds increasing to a gusty 30 to 50 mph, creating areas of blowing snow and visibility less than a half mile at times, as well as dangerously cold wind chills by late afternoon and evening of December 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After one to three inches of snowfall, winds increased to 35 to 45 mph as temperatures plummeted, taking wind chill values as low as -23 at Chamberlain during the evening hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated to 1 to 2 inches, and while winds gusted as high as 39 mph at Parkston and 52 mph near Geddes, periods of blowing snow with visibility of one-half mile or less occurred between 0900-1700CST.  Travel was not recommended due to near-whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27.  A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN.  This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28.  A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN.||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing briefly to light freezing rain and rain from 1600-0000CST.  Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times.  Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 35 to 45 mph from 1500CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28.  Snowfall accumulated to 7.1 inches near Chester and 6.0 inches at Madison with a peak wind gust of 48 mph. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27.  A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN.  This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28.  A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN.||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and light mixed precipitation developed in the early morning on December 26, changing to light rain around 1200CST. Snow redeveloped around 0000CST December 27, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27. Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 40 to 50 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28. Snowfall accumulated to 8.0 inches 2 miles south of Armour, with a peak wind gust of 48 mph near Geddes and 41 mph at Parkston, along with several hours of visibility of one half mile or less. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

An arctic front surged southward early in the day with winds increasing to a gusty 30 to 50 mph, creating areas of blowing snow and visibility less than a half mile at times, as well as dangerously cold wind chills by late afternoon and evening of December 31.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated to 1 to 2 inches, and while winds gusted as high as 42 mph at Yankton and 36 mph near Beresford, periods of blowing snow with visibility of one-half mile or less occurred between 1100-1800CST.  Travel was not recommended due to near-whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27.  A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN.  This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28.  A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN.||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and light mixed precipitation developed early morning on December 26, changing to light rain around 1200CST. Snow redeveloped around 0000CST December 27, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27. Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 40 to 50 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28. Snowfall accumulated to 9.2 inches at White Lake, 9.1 inches at Stickney and 8.5 inches at Plankinton, with a peak wind gust of 47 mph at White Lake, along with several hours of visibility of one half mile or less. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing briefly to light freezing rain and rain from 1800-2300CST.  Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times.  Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 35 to 45 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28.  Snowfall accumulated to 7.3 inches at Iroquois and 7.0 inches at De Smet with a peak wind gust of 46 mph. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2.  The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning.  Heavy snow and wind developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to Interstate 29 in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain which produced a glaze of light icing during the early morning of December 1, heavy snow occurred for numerous hours. Accumulations over less than a 24-hour period totaled 7.0 inches at Menno and 6.5 inches at Parkston. Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce several hours of visibility of a half mile or less along with treacherous travel.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing to light rain from 1200-0200CST.  Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon and early evening of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times.  Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 35 to 45 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28.  Snowfall accumulated to 8.5 inches at Yankton with a peak wind gust of 45 mph. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27.  A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN.  This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28.  A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN.||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing briefly to light freezing rain or rain from 1200-0000CST.  Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times.  Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 35 to 45 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28.  Snowfall accumulated to 6.5 inches at Menno and Parkston with a peak wind gust of 41 mph at Parkston. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a shortwave disturbance rotated around a large southwest U.S. closed low aloft and into confluent flow aloft across the Northern Plains, light wintry precipitation developed on December 31.  An arctic front surged southward early in the day with winds increasing to a gusty 30 to 50 mph, creating areas of blowing snow and visibility less than a half mile at times, as well as dangerously cold wind chills by late afternoon and evening of December 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After one to two inches of snowfall, winds increased to 35 to 50 mph as temperatures plummeted, taking wind chill values as low as -23 near Geddes during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system and strong cold front crossed the region. Temperatures were cold enough for snow across the Black Hills area. The heaviest snow fell over the southern Black Hills, where five to ten inches were reported.  Lesser amounts fell across the rest of the Black Hills and far southwestern South Dakota.

A strong upper-level wave and jet entrance region pushed into the Northern Plains, with a tight mid-level frontal boundary stalling across the area for several hours within confluent southwest flow aloft.  Unseasonably cold air rushed into the region behind a southward surging low-level frontal boundary,  with developing precipitation making a very rapid transition from rain to snow.  Snow accumulated quickly in the overnight and early morning hours due to proximity of slightly unstable air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snow accumulated to 2 to 6 inches across portions of southeast South Dakota near and south of Interstate 90. Higher totals measured were 7 miles east of Fairfax (6.0 inches), 4 miles southwest of Burke (4.0 inches), 8 miles southwest of Herrick (4.0 inches), and near Bonesteel (3.0 inches).

A significant surface low pressure area lifted northeast across the region and brought widespread wet snowfall to central and northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 5 inch range with some locally heavy amounts of 6 to 9 inches. Along with the snowfall came strong north winds resulting in the buildup of snow on trees and power lines. As a result, over a thousand customers were without power through the day and several hundred remained without power overnight. Spotty tree damage also occurred. The heavy snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Westport and Hecla, 7 inches at Claremont, and 9 inches at Britton.

The severe drought conditions in September continued into early October before finally improving by mid October. Several rounds of rainfall along with some snowfall resulted in the improving drought conditions.
High releases continued from upstream dams along the Missouri River and kept much of the stretch between Fort Randall Dam and Gavins Point Dam in minor flood stage for most of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increased releases from Fort Randall Dam resulted in a return to minor flooding of mainly agricultural areas at the start of October. The Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2) crested at 0.3 feet above flood stage on October 3. The river dropped below flood stage on October 7, but would return above flood stage a couple more times during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases continued from upstream dams along the Missouri River and kept much of the stretch between Fort Randall Dam and Gavins Point Dam in minor flood stage for most of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Other than a brief period from October 8-11, minor flooding of mainly agricultural areas persisted through the entire month along the Missouri River near Niobrara (gage site MRNN1). The river crested at 1.1 feet above flood stage on October 3, and again 1.2 feet above flood stage on October 13. The river remained above flood stage at the end of the month.

The river near Davis (gage site DVSS2) crested 0.3 feet above flood stage on October 12, producing flooding of agricultural lands near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued to ease on portions of the Big Sioux River from around Akron southward to near Richland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from September, river levels on the Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) continued to fall in early October. Impacts were limited to minor agricultural flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle produced treacherous driving conditions by early afternoon.  Several accidents resulted from the glaze of ice on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. Gradual loss of deep saturation resulted in a trailing area of light freezing rain or freezing drizzle behind the main light snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a very brief period of light snow, periods of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle resulted in treacherous road conditions with up to a few hundredths of an inch accumulation.  One fatality and four injuries (indirect) occurred the following morning when a vehicle lost control on an icy bridge and rolled.
